Ungraded problem sets and solutions will be provided to provide focus to this activity Formal coverage of the fundamentals of aerospace propulsion and combustion systems and examples of their application to a wide variety of systems The 2 hour workshop sessions begin with a brief demonstration of applying the course content to analyze a propulsion or combustion system, after which short, open book questions are to be completed in small groups. While discussion is strongly encouraged, each student should write their own version of the group's solution. During a presentation of the solution and class discussion, students should reflect on their solution, identifying errors and how they might be remedied. This process ensures you receive regular, timely feedback on your learning. This will be followed by informal discussions with course staff on any issues relating to course, with particular emphasis on applying lecture material to solve practical aerospace propulsion problems Rocket thrust measurement Experiments to be performed in groups of 4 in scheduled laboratory class times | Understand the operating principles of air-breathing propulsion systems, rockets and combustion systems Analyse aircraft and spacecraft engines by synthesising concepts introduced in fluid mechanics and thermodynamics to develop system models Analyse combustion systems by synthesising concepts introduced in chemistry, fluid mechanics and thermodynamics to develop system models Predict and evaluate the performance of new and novel engine designs using the analysis techniques described above Justify which engine performance measures are most relevant for different applications Design an optimal propulsion system for a given application, using appropriate performance measures to rationally select the system type and optimise its parameters Experimentally investigate propulsion system performance as a team and present findings as a professional engineering report.

title: Media Planning and Buying

assessments.task: Assessment 3 (Part A): AI-Assisted Integrated Media Plan (Group) Team or group-based
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Project

assessments.task: Assessment 3 (Part B) Team Pitch - AI Assisted Integrated Media Plan (Group)  Team or group-based

description: The business of advertising is driven by large global advertisers and the media companies supporting and implementing their media planning and plans. This course provides an overview of the processes of media buying and planning with an emphasis on the design and implementation of strategic media plans. Media companies are major employers within the advertising industry and today they dominate the advertising and media landscape. This comprehensive course serves as an introduction to the world of media planning and buying via readings and in-seminar exercises. This course focuses on understanding media plans, media choices, campaign scheduling, and nominally quantifiable costs. Assessments will involve practical media and campaign planning tasks which reinforce the course content as they will require an understanding of media characteristics and audience measurement. Student learning may also be augmented by a guest lecture (subject to availability) from an advertising or media planning practitioner. At the completion of the course students will have gained a general understanding of the extensive range of issues and challenges involved in media selection and buying in the dynamic marketing environment.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of the role and importance of media buying and planning in the strategic advertising process.
Evaluate the characteristics and strengths of specific media channels and vehicles to reach desired target audiences.
Articulate the importance of advertising strategy and consumer behaviour in generating a successful media campaign.
Gain a deep level of knowledge of the various advertising media available, including features, trends and measurement.
Establish and apply media strategies to achieve strategic marketing objectives both effectively and cost efficiently in response to a client brief.

title: Food for a Healthy Planet

assessments.task: Oral Group Presentation Team or group-based

description: The course addresses a pertinent challenge of humankind: how to feed 12 billion people while maintaining the integrity and function of our planet. It challenges participants with contrasting viewpoints for a nuanced understanding of the multidimensional aspects of food production and consumption. Course participants explore the food debate as consumers and scholars, with focus on the science behind innovation of food and food systems, locally and globally. Course participants map the future of food and agriculture with view of the UN's Sustainable Development Goals. Food is central to life. There are many ways to satisfy our nutritional needs and we should make decisions with our health in mind. We also have to consider environmental health - how much land, water and other resources are used? How do our dietary choices affect the footprint of food production?
learning_outcomes: Explain the impacts of agriculture in the context of human and environmental health.
Illustrate the processes involved in food consumption and food production.
Collaborate effectively with peers and experts.
Collate, critically evaluate and communicate scientific knowledge.
topics: Series Please refer to the full timetable in Blackboard Sub-activity: Course Introduction - what is food, where does it come from, how do we make more with less? Food Productivity; Animal Productivity; Plant Productivity; Course Summary - highlighting the many commonalities shared between food, animal and plant production. How can we improve food production and reduce the inputs and costs associated with it?

title: Advanced Research Methodologies
description: This course encompasses both qualitative and quantitative research and analysis. Students will gain an understanding of the skills needed to design and undertake a research project, including the legal and ethical requirements in planning research projects. The course covers choosing the best experimental design and analytical methods, as well as how to present data for dissemination to the wider community. This course provides students with the framework of principles and methods for defining research issues, developing research proposals, and communicating research progress in both oral and written formats. It helps students understand the 'big picture' of research and development within agricultural settings.
learning_outcomes: Understand the complexity and validity of diverse research approaches from biological to social sciences, while emphasising fundamental research principles including academic integrity.
Plan research projects and apply the principles of the research process to problems in the fields of Agriculture and Food Science.
Clearly articulate research questions and assemble evidence to support development of a research project to address this question.
Effectively communicate complex ideas to diverse audiences.
topics: and Presentations This course includes a series of guest lectures covering a broad range of topics. Lectures will be delivered by experts either in person or online, with additional short video lectures available on Blackboard to support in-depth tutorials across various disciplines in agriculture and food research. The lecture timetable will be accessible on the Blackboard site. The course coordinator will communicate regularly with students through Blackboard announcements to keep them informed about upcoming activities and guest lectures

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 12
learning_activities.topic: and Presentations This course includes a series of guest lectures covering a broad range of topics. Lectures will be delivered by experts either in person or online, with additional short video lectures available on Blackboard to support in-depth tutorials across various disciplines in agriculture and food research. The lecture timetable will be accessible on the Blackboard site. The course coordinator will communicate regularly with students through Blackboard announcements to keep them informed about upcoming activities and guest lectures

Tutorials (Tutorial Series): This course covers two main modules: Fundamentals of Research (Weeks 1-5) and Discipline-Related Tutorials (Weeks 6-12). The tutorials include Q&A sessions, workshops, discussions, and focus groups to deepen the understanding of each topic. The session in Module 1 introduces the basics of how to conduct research. Students are expected to attend and participate in these sessions. We will cover a variety of discipline-related learning activities, including those from social sciences, agribusiness, soil sciences, agronomy, crop protection, animal sciences, and food sciences. For more details about the modules and learning activities, please visit the Blackboard site. Guest lecturers from each discipline will lead these sessions. The timetable will be accessible on the Blackboard site. The course coordinator will communicate regularly with students through Blackboard announcements to inform them about upcoming activities and guest lectures

learning_activities.topic: 3-Minute Pitch Style Presentation (3MP) 3-Minute Pitch Style Presentation (3MP) of a Project Proposal (Seminar): During weeks 10-13, each student will present a very short (maximum 3-minute) seminar to explain their project proposal and its significance. The goal is to make the proposal appealing to a general audience with basic knowledge of agriculture or food science. Due to the high number of students enrolled in this course, this activity might begin in Week 10, at the end of each tutorial. Students will be selected and allocated to each session randomly for their 3MP presentations

title: Systematic Anatomy
description: Structures of the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine, cardiiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems will be examined. This systemic anatomy course provides students with an opportunity to develop an understanding of the interdependence of structure and function of human anatomy at the level of:
learning_outcomes: ANATOMICAL TERMINOLOGY: Communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology.
ANATOMICAL IDENTIFICATION: Identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of each of the major human organ systems on real human anatomical (cadaveric) specimens.
ANATOMICAL STRUCTURE: Describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute each major human organ system.
ANATOMICAL RELATIONSHIPS: Summarise the relationships between the various organ systems.
ANATOMICAL ORGAN FUNCTION: Define the principal function/s of each major human organ system.
FORM-FUNCTION INTERDEPENDENCE: Apply the concept of interdependent 'form and function' to illustrate how the structure of an organ system accomplishes its functions.
STRUCTURAL & FUNCTIONAL CHANGE: Demonstrate the ability to predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure.
topics: COURSE INTRO I: OVERVIEW [Glen] Introduction to the course activities, required materials and assessment. Introduction to systemic anatomy, main themes and concepts
COURSE INTRO II: Anatomical terminology, concepts and organisation [Glen] Anatomical terminology & concepts. Anatomical Organisation (Cells > tissues > organs > organ systems > organism)

learning_activities.topic: MODULE A: INTRODUCTION TO THIS COURSE This introductory mini-module informs the students of important rules and regulations involved when studying anatomy, and some key anatomical concepts and terminology te get started

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 1 - SKELETAL SYSTEM (Osteology & Arthrology) [Glen] WEEKS 1-4 This module introduces the student to the study of bones (osteology) and joints (arthrology), and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the skeletal system. As the skeleton forms the framework of the human body, this module also familiarises the student with general structure, arrangement and movements of the body. A detailed understanding of the structure and function of the bones and joints, and organisation of the skeletal system is particularly important foundational knowledge for learning the muscular system in the next module, since the muscles are attached to these skeletal structures and drive their movements about these joints
COURSE INTRO I: OVERVIEW [Glen] Introduction to the course activities, required materials and assessment. Introduction to systemic anatomy, main themes and concepts
COURSE INTRO II: Anatomical terminology, concepts and organisation [Glen] Anatomical terminology & concepts. Anatomical Organisation (Cells > tissues > organs > organ systems > organism)

GAF F2F Induction (mandatory) Mandatory "Face-to-face" (F2F) Induction to the Gross Anatomy Facility (GAF) . IMPORTANT: Students must attend the GAF F2F induction to be permitted entry to the GAF for practical classes. (See "8. Learning Resources" and Blackboard): (1) All students must first complete the Online Anatomy Induction 72hours (i.e. 3 full days) before this F2F induction. (2) All students must bring correct PPE (labcoat, safety glasses, covered shoes) and UQ Student ID card

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 6 (09 Mar - 05 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: MODULE 2 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM (Myology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of muscles (myology), and comprehensively examines the structure and function most of the of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the muscular system. As the muscular system drives and coordinates the movement and orientation of the body, and assists the skeleton in support and protection, this module also further familiarises the student with the general structure, arrangement and movements of the body, as well as develops an understanding of how these movements are caused and coordinated. A detailed knowledge of the attachment and arrangement of the muscles relative to the bones and joints of the skeleton (covered in the previous module) is important foundational knowledge for understanding how muscles and groups of muscles generate and coordinate movement and orientation of the body

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 3 - NERVOUS & ENDOCRINE SYSTEMS (Neurology & Endocrinology) [Aland] This module introduces the student to the study of the nervous system (neurology) and endocrine system (endocrinology) from an anatomical perspective, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these two systems. Students will learn that both systems are involved in sending signals from one part of the body to another (and also interact with each other): the nervous system can send extremely fast and precisely targeted electrochemical signals from one end of the body to the other; whereas the endocrine system generally broadcasts chemical signals through fluids of the body that trigger responses in tissues sensitive to them. This knowledge is foundational for the understanding of how the anatomy of the nervous system' relates to its role in the coordination and processing of sensory input, coordination of movement of the musculoskeletal system, and its signalling to key physiological rates and states; and understanding how the anatomical arrangement of endocrine organs relate to their function

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 4 - VISCERAL SYSTEMS (Splanchnology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of the visceral systems (splanchnology) which includes the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these. "Viscera" refers to the soft internal organs housed centrally in the body, but note that the cardiovascular and lymphatic systems include vessels that branch out into the extremities. The student will learn how the anatomy of these organs relate to their function

search_blob: ANAT1020 Systematic Anatomy | Structures of the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine, cardiiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems will be examined. This systemic anatomy course provides students with an opportunity to develop an understanding of the interdependence of structure and function of human anatomy at the level of: | Student Identification (ID) Card - organise ASAP Student ID Card is required for scan-in to gain entry to the Gross Anatomy Facility (GAF) to attend inductions and anatomy practicals, due to State and UQ regulations. Because of potential delays in processing and producing Student ID's (up to 14 days), we strongly urge all students to organise these well before Oweek starts so you can have it ready to use on Monday of Week 1 Anatomy Online Inductions (Mandatory) Online Anatomy Inductions must be completed by Friday in Oweek to be permitted into the Gross Anatomy Facility (GAF) to attend the GAF F2F Induction in Week 1 and all subsequent anatomy pracs in the GAF. Enrolled students will be informed of details and directions to access link via Blackboard (Bb) MODULE A: INTRODUCTION TO THIS COURSE This introductory mini-module informs the students of important rules and regulations involved when studying anatomy, and some key anatomical concepts and terminology te get started MODULE 1 - SKELETAL SYSTEM (Osteology & Arthrology) [Glen] WEEKS 1-4 This module introduces the student to the study of bones (osteology) and joints (arthrology), and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the skeletal system. As the skeleton forms the framework of the human body, this module also familiarises the student with general structure, arrangement and movements of the body. A detailed understanding of the structure and function of the bones and joints, and organisation of the skeletal system is particularly important foundational knowledge for learning the muscular system in the next module, since the muscles are attached to these skeletal structures and drive their movements about these joints COURSE INTRO I: OVERVIEW [Glen] Introduction to the course activities, required materials and assessment. Introduction to systemic anatomy, main themes and concepts COURSE INTRO II: Anatomical terminology, concepts and organisation [Glen] Anatomical terminology & concepts. Anatomical Organisation (Cells > tissues > organs > organ systems > organism) GAF F2F Induction (mandatory) Mandatory "Face-to-face" (F2F) Induction to the Gross Anatomy Facility (GAF) . IMPORTANT: Students must attend the GAF F2F induction to be permitted entry to the GAF for practical classes. (See "8. Learning Resources" and Blackboard): (1) All students must first complete the Online Anatomy Induction 72hours (i.e. 3 full days) before this F2F induction. (2) All students must bring correct PPE (labcoat, safety glasses, covered shoes) and UQ Student ID card SKELETAL SYSTEM I: Introduction [Glen] Components, functions, development, growth and maintenance of the skeletal system A: How to learn Anatomy [Glen] SKELETAL SYSTEM II: Axial Skeleton 1 - The Skull & Hyoid [Glen] SKELETAL SYSTEM III: Axial Skeleton 2 - Postcranial Skeleton [Glen] PRAC 1.1 - SKELETAL SYSTEM A: Axial Skeleton [Glen] AXIAL SKELETON - Skull & Postcranial Skeleton. Also a general introduction to the skeletal system, its development and growth, and the classification of bones SKELETAL SYSTEM IV: Apendicular Skeleton [Glen] The Appendicular Skeleton 1.1: Skeletal System (Q&A & Review) [Glen] MODULE 2 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM (Myology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of muscles (myology), and comprehensively examines the structure and function most of the of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the muscular system. As the muscular system drives and coordinates the movement and orientation of the body, and assists the skeleton in support and protection, this module also further familiarises the student with the general structure, arrangement and movements of the body, as well as develops an understanding of how these movements are caused and coordinated. A detailed knowledge of the attachment and arrangement of the muscles relative to the bones and joints of the skeleton (covered in the previous module) is important foundational knowledge for understanding how muscles and groups of muscles generate and coordinate movement and orientation of the body SKELETAL SYSTEM V: Arthrology 1 - General joint structure, function & classification [Glen] Arthrology Basics - structural and functional classifications of all types of joints (bony, cartilaginous, fibrous & synovial joints) SKELETAL SYSTEM VI: Arthrology 2 - Synovial joints [Glen] Special Focus: Synovial Joints (structure & function) MUSCULAR SYSTEM I: Muscle tissues & organs - structure & function [Glen] Skeletal muscle organ structure, muscle contraction, motor units and muscle architecture. Introduction to biomechanics PRAC 1.2 - SKELETAL SYSTEM B: Upper Appendicular Skeleton [Glen] UPPER APPENDICULAR SKELETON - Osteology & Arthrology MUSCULAR SYSTEM II: Axial muscles - Head, Neck & Torso [Glen] MUSCULAR SYSTEM III: Appendicular Muscles 1 - Shoulder & Arm [Glen] PRAC 1.3 - SKELETAL SYSTEM C: Lower Appendicular Skeleton [Glen] LOWER APPENDICULAR SKELETON - Osteology & Arthrology MUSCULAR SYSTEM IV: Appendicular Muscles 2 - Forearm & Hand [Glen] Muscles of the forearm & hand 2.1: Axial Muscles (Q&A & Review) [Glen] MUSCULAR SYSTEM V: Muscles of the Hip & Thigh [Glen] Muscles of the hip & thigh MUSCULAR SYSTEM VI: Muscles of the Leg & Foot [Glen] Muscles of the leg & foot PRAC 2.1 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM A: Axial Muscles [Glen] AXIAL MUSCLES - muscles of the head, neck & trunk PRAC 2.2 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM B: Upper Appendicular Muscles [Glen] UPPER APPENDICULAR MUSCLES - pectoral girdle & upper limb PRAC 2.3 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM C: Lower Appendicular Muscles [Glen] LOWER APPENDICULAR MUSCLES - pelvic girdle & lower limb MID-SEMESTER REVISION PRAC: Musculoskeletal Systems 2.2: Appendicular Muscles (Q&A & Review) MODULE 3 - NERVOUS & ENDOCRINE SYSTEMS (Neurology & Endocrinology) [Aland] This module introduces the student to the study of the nervous system (neurology) and endocrine system (endocrinology) from an anatomical perspective, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these two systems. Students will learn that both systems are involved in sending signals from one part of the body to another (and also interact with each other): the nervous system can send extremely fast and precisely targeted electrochemical signals from one end of the body to the other; whereas the endocrine system generally broadcasts chemical signals through fluids of the body that trigger responses in tissues sensitive to them. This knowledge is foundational for the understanding of how the anatomy of the nervous system' relates to its role in the coordination and processing of sensory input, coordination of movement of the musculoskeletal system, and its signalling to key physiological rates and states; and understanding how the anatomical arrangement of endocrine organs relate to their function NERVOUS SYSTEM I: Central Nervous System (CNS) 1 - (details TBA) [Aland] Central Nervous System (CNS) - the brain and spinal cord NERVOUS SYSTEM II: Central Nervous System (CNS) 2 - (details TBA) [Aland] PRAC 3.1 - NERVOUS SYSTEM A - CNS: Brain, Ventricular System & Meninges [Aland] NERVOUS SYSTEM III: Central Nervous System (CNS) 3 - (details TBA) [Aland] NERVOUS SYSTEM IV: Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) 1 - Cranial Nerves [Aland] Peripheral nervous system (PNS) 1 - cranial nerves (12 pairs) NERVOUS SYSTEM V: Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) 2 - Spinal Nerves [Aland] Peripheral nervous system (PNS) 2 - nerve roots, rami & spinal nerves PRAC 3.2 - NERVOUS SYSTEM B - CNS: Spinal Cord & PNS: Cranial Nerves [Aland] NERVOUS SYSTEM VI: Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) 3 - plexuses, branches, and peripheral nerve innervation [Aland] Peripheral nervous system (PNS) 3 - nerve plexuses & branches NERVOUS SYSTEM VII: Special Senses [Aland] Summary of the structure & function of the somatic nervous system & special senses 3.1: Nervous System 1 - CNS & PNS [Aland] CNS - brain & spinal cord PNS - Cranial nerves NERVOUS SYSTEM VIII: Autonomic Nervous System (ANS) & Endocrine System [Aland] PRAC 3.3 - NERVOUS SYSTEM C - PNS: Spinal Nerves, Plexuses & Peripheral Innervation [Aland] 3.2: Nervous System 2 & Endocrine System [Aland] PNS - Spinal nerves, special senses; ANS Endocrine System MODULE 4 - VISCERAL SYSTEMS (Splanchnology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of the visceral systems (splanchnology) which includes the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these. "Viscera" refers to the soft internal organs housed centrally in the body, but note that the cardiovascular and lymphatic systems include vessels that branch out into the extremities. The student will learn how the anatomy of these organs relate to their function CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM I: The Heart [Glen] Introduction & the heart CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM II: Blood Vessels & Lymphatics [Glen] Blood vessels & the lymphatic system RESPIRATORY SYSTEM [Glen] Structure and functional anatomy of the respiratory system DIGESTIVE SYSTEM I: Upper DIgestive Systems & Salivary Glands [Glen] Gastrointestinal Tract (GIT) in the head and neck, plus associated accessory organs (salivary gland) DIGESTIVE SYSTEM II: Lower Digestive System & Associated Accessory Organs [Glen] Gastrointestinal Tract (GIT) in the torso, plus associated accessory organs (liver, gallbladder & pancreas) PRAC 4.1 - VISCERAL SYSTEMS A: Cardiorespiratory Systems [Glen] CARDIORESPIRATORY SYSTEMS: Cardiovascular System (CVS) Respiratory System URINARY SYSTEM [Glen] REPRODUCTIVE SYSTEM: Male & Female [Glen] Male reproductive system Revision Lectorial [Glen] PRAC 4.2 - VISCERAL SYSTEMS B: Abdominopelvic Visceral Systems [Glen] Visceral systems of the abdominopelvic cavity: Digestive System Urinary System Reproductive System 4.1: Visceral Systems Visceral Systems of the thoracic & abdominopelvic cavity (revision) | ANATOMICAL TERMINOLOGY: Communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology. ANATOMICAL IDENTIFICATION: Identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of each of the major human organ systems on real human anatomical (cadaveric) specimens. ANATOMICAL STRUCTURE: Describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute each major human organ system. ANATOMICAL RELATIONSHIPS: Summarise the relationships between the various organ systems. ANATOMICAL ORGAN FUNCTION: Define the principal function/s of each major human organ system. FORM-FUNCTION INTERDEPENDENCE: Apply the concept of interdependent 'form and function' to illustrate how the structure of an organ system accomplishes its functions. STRUCTURAL & FUNCTIONAL CHANGE: Demonstrate the ability to predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure.

title: Functional Anatomy (Physiotherapy)

assessments.task: Invigilated End of Semester Exam    Online

description: This course is designed to provide continued study of the musculoskeletal and nervous systems in relation to their function and dysfunction in human movement. This course is designed to provide continued study of human musculoskeletal and nervous systems, in relation to their function and dysfunction, in movement. In addition to the normal mechanical behaviour of anatomical structures comprising these systems, we investigate theﾠresponses ofﾠmusculoskeletal and nervous system structures to different loading conditions, injury, disease, and rehabilitation. Normal mechanisms of postural and movement control are examined along with changes that occur during aging and/or pathological conditions.
learning_outcomes: Define basic mechanical terms and concepts.
Use correct terminology to describe the mechanical response of human nervous and musculoskeletal structures to normal or altered loading conditions.
Predict and explain the response of human nervous and musculoskeletal structures to insufficient or excessive mechanical loading.
Identify the appropriate imaging modality for various nervous and musculoskeletal structures or pathologies.
Relate the normal structure and function of the skeletal, muscular and nervous systems to normal human posture and movement.
topics: Introduction to course and to basic biomechanics [Langfield] In-person on-campus lecture. Overview and discussion of course aims, learning activities, assessment and expectations. Introduction to basic biomechanical principles

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to course and to basic biomechanics [Langfield] In-person on-campus lecture. Overview and discussion of course aims, learning activities, assessment and expectations. Introduction to basic biomechanical principles

Module 1: Introduction to biomechanics [Langfield] A combination of lecture and online interactive module provide an introduction to basic biomechanical terminology and concepts applicable to this course. Students complete the learning activities at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the tutorial on the following Monday. Estimated time 2hrs
Module 1: Introduction to biomechanics tutorial [Langfield]

Module 2: Biomechanics of bone [Lang] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 3hrs) 1. Structure (micro- and macro-) and mechanical behaviour of mature cortical & trabecular bone. 2. The biomechanics of bone through the stages of development, maturity & aging. Modelling and remodelling. Gender differences. How bone responds to normal mechanical loading. 3.Pathological response of bone to insufficient or excessive mechanical forces. Biomechanical explanation for types of fractures / bone pathologies. Biomechanical rationale for rehabilitation
Module 2: Biomechanics of bone tutorial [Lang]

Module 3: Synovial joint biomechanics [Langfield] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 3hrs) 1. Biomechanics and lubrication of synovial joints. 2. Compare and contrast the structure and mechanical behaviour of hyaline cartilage and fibrocartilage. Tibiofemoral meniscal pathology. 3.Aging and pathology of synovial joints
Module 3: Synovial joint biomechanics tutorial [Langfield]

Module 4: Biomechanics of tendons & ligaments [Williamson] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 3hrs) 1. Structure and mechanical behaviour of tendons and ligaments, entheses & enthesis organs. How variation in tendon and ligament structure affects the biomechanics. 2. The biomechanics of tendon and ligament through the stages of maturity & aging. Gender differences. Response to normal mechanical loading. 3.Response of tendon and ligament to excessive loading or unloading. Biomechanical rationale for rehabilitation
Module 4: Tendon & ligament tutorial [Williamson]

Module 5: Musculotendinous unit and EMG [Williamson] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 4hrs) 1. Muscle architecture, fibre length, moment arm, PCSA, force-length curve, force-velocity curve, passive & active contributors to production of torque. 2. The biomechanics of the musculotendinous unit through the stages of maturity & aging. Response to normal mechanical loading. 3. Response of skeletal muscle to excessive loading or unloading. Biomechanical rationale for rehabilitation. 4. Measuring muscle activity to advance our knowledge of movement control. Research & clinical applications of electromyography; understanding electromechanical delay
Module 5: Musculoskeletal Unit and EMG tutorial [Williamson]

Module 6: Musculoskeletal imaging [Lang] Students progress through the online pre-recorded lecture at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 2hrs) Review of the various forms of imaging used to assess musculoskeletal injuries and those used in research
Module 6: Musculoskeletal Imaging tutorial [Lang]

Module 7: Peripheral Nerve Biomechanics [Langfield] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 3hrs) 1.Structure and biomechanics of peripheral nerves. 2.Peripheral nerve injuries. 3.Biomechanics relevant to rehabilitation
Module 7: Peripheral nerve biomechanics tutorial [Langfield]

Module 8: Biomechanics of the brain [Paolino] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 4hrs) 1. Biomechanics of the brain and relate. 2. Acquired Brain Injuries - Traumatic brain injury mechanisms and pathologies. 3. Acquired Brain Injuries - The classification and pathology of stroke. 4. Primary and secondary pathology of acquired central nervous system injuries and treatment
Module 8: Nervous system GAF practical [Paolino] Examination of normal and pathological brain and spinal cord specimens. Relating anatomical pathology to dysfunction

Module 9: Motor control [Williamson] Students progress through a series of online pre-recorded lectures at their preferred pace and time to prepare for the related tutorial scheduled the following Monday. (estimated time 3hrs) 1.Control of posture and movement. 2.Corticomotor plasticity & transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS). 3.Motor adaptation to pain: Conference presentation: How does acute pain influence the control of single motor units and whole body segments?
Module 9: Motor Control Tutorial [Williamson]

title: Social Media Management

assessments.task: Social Media Marketing Campaign Part A Online
Social Media Marketing Campaign Part B Online
Google Garage: Digital Marketing Accreditation  Online

description: Social media is the fastest growing area of advertising expenditure worldwide. Advertisers are increasingly shifting their advertising expenditure to social media at the expense of traditional media formats (especially broadcast media). This course provides students with an introduction to the effective use of social media by advertising and places emphasis on planning and evaluation of social media strategies. Employment opportunities in social media are rapidly growing. This course is designed to prepare students for professional careers in industry. Building on their prior foundational theoretical advertising knowledge, this course focuses on giving students practical social media management experience and accreditation.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate understanding of the role of social media in the overall marketing strategy for the business.
Critically evaluate the effectiveness of various social media platforms and strategies.
Explain different metrics and ways to measure performance to achieve tactics.
Design and implement a portfolio with various social media marketing strategies and/or tactics.
Identify, analyse and resolve social marketing management issues and risks.

learning_activities.topic: The Social Media Environment
Social Consumers
Network Structure and Group Influences in Social Media
Social Media Marketing Strategy
Tactical Planning and Execution
Content Creation and Design Good Friday Public Holiday - Friday 3rd April 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes

Social Community (Zone 1)
Social Publishing (Zone 2)
Social Entertainment (Zone 3)
Social Commerce (Zone 4)
Social Media Analytics and Metrics
Social Media Policy and Crisis Response
Wrap-up & Final Assessment Q&A

title: Functional Musculoskeletal Anatomy - Human Movement
description: This course provides a broad overview of human functional anatomy with a focus on the muscular and skeletal systems. Topics include i) tissue organization and mechanical properties; ii) integration of the muscular, skeletal & nervous systems for movement; and iii) the influence of maturation, ageing, exercise, immobilisation and injury on components of the musculoskeletal system. This course provides a comprehensiveﾠoverview of human musculoskeletal structure and function. Learning activities examine the microstructure, biomechanics, and gross anatomy of the ﾠhuman musculoskeletal systems, in relation toﾠhumanﾠposture and movement. The effects of development, maturation, ageing, exercise, immobilisation and injury on the musculoskeletal system are studied.
learning_outcomes: Identify musculoskeletal structures and peripheral nerves on human cadaveric specimens and/or images.
Describe the relationship between the anatomy and function of the skeletal, muscular and nervous systems in the various regions of the human body.
Relate the biomechanics of the musculoskeletal system, at the micro and macro levels of organisation, to human movement and mechanisms of injury.
Explain how skeletal and musculotendinous anatomy is affected by exercise, immobilisation, disease, and aging.
Integrate knowledge of the interdependence of normal anatomy and function to predict and explain functional deficits resulting from altered anatomy.
topics: Course introduction [Langfield] On campus in-person lecture to outline course aims & expected outcomes, learning activities, and assessment

learning_activities.topic: Tissue biomechanics online pre-recorded lectures Tissue biomechanics lectures are online pre-recorded lectures that students complete at their preferred time and pace before the corresponding 'Tissue biomechanics' tutorial. Each set of lectures will be made available 1 week (on Friday) prior to the related tutorial (the following Friday). Reading material will also be available through the UQ library
Course introduction [Langfield] On campus in-person lecture to outline course aims & expected outcomes, learning activities, and assessment

Regional Anatomy GAF onsite induction There is one mandatory GAF induction session scheduled in week 1. Students must complete this induction to be permitted entry to the Gross Anatomy Facility for practical classes and exams

learning_activities.topic: Tissue biomechanics tutorials Three in-person, on campus Tissue biomechanics tutorials are timetabled during weeks 2 to 4. Tutorials provide an opportunity for extensive student engagement. The Tissue Biomechanics tutorials include both interactive and collaborative activities whereby students further their understanding of course theoretical content. Reading material will also be available through the UQ library

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 13 (16 Mar - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Regional Anatomy UQ Extend modules Regional Anatomy theory content will be made available via Blackboard UQ Extend modules allowing you to self-pace your learning and complete formative exercises to receive personalised feedback and measure your understanding of course content prior to the related practical class. You should allocate a minimum of 3 to 4 hours per week to complete the UQ Extend content PRIOR to the related GAF practical

Practicals allow students to view and identify anatomical structure, normal anatomical variation, and specific pathologies. These highly interactive practical classes also provide opportunity for students to complete formative assessment tasks and to engage in discussions with academics and demonstrators to receive feedback on learning and further their understanding of course theoretical content

Relate the biomechanics of the musculoskeletal system, at the micro and macro levels of organisation, to human movement and mechanisms of injury. Explain how skeletal and musculotendinous anatomy is affected by exercise, immobilisation, disease, and aging. Integrate knowledge of the interdependence of normal anatomy and function to predict and explain functional deficits resulting from altered anatomy.

title: The Rise of Ancient Greece: Greek History to the 4th Century BC
description: A survey of early Greek history and society in the light of literary and archaeological evidence. The course commences with the Bronze Age civilizations of Crete and Mycenae and extends to the fall of classical Athens in 404 BC. ﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠ This first-year course of 3 contact hours per week explores the fascinating political history of ancient Greece from 1200 to 300 BC. The Rise of Ancient Greece: Greek History to the Fourth Century BC focusses on the rise of the Greek city-states in the archaic period, their creation of political institutions, their formation of multilateral military alliances, and their wars among themselves and against the Persian empire. It considers all the major conflicts of the eastern Mediterranean during the late archaic and classical periods as well as what impact different political systems had on the warmaking of the Greek and Persian belligerents. The Rise of Ancient Greece: Greek History to the Fourth Century BC also explores the rich society and culture of the Greeks in classical times. It explores in detail the place of women in classical Athens and classical Sparta as well the invention of Greek theatre. The Rise of Ancient Greece: Greek History to the Fourth Century BC studies major literary genres that the ancient Greeks invented as well as important aspects of ancient Greek art. The course trains students in standard methods of Ancient History and helps them to become excellent writers of their own historical research.ﾠ
learning_outcomes: apply historical method to a range of important questions in Greek history through critical analysis of ancient evidence and modern opinion and through the construction of detailed, reasoned arguments.
recognise distinctive features of the Bronze, Archaic and Classical Ages of Ancient Greece.
recognise and contextualise a range of famous events, personalities, writers and material remains of Ancient Greece
relate the lecture topics to one another in a meaningful, developmental way.
topics: Introductory Lecture
1. Early Greece
2. The Making of the Archaic Greek City-States
3. Greece Confronts Persia: The Ionian Revolt and the Persian Wars
4. The Fifty-Year Estrangement of Athens and Sparta
5. The Dramatic Festivals of Dionysus: Comedy and Tragedy
6. The Peloponnesian War Begins: From the Outbreak to the Catastrophe in Sicily

8. Sparta Becomes Athens: The Peloponnesian War's Last Ten Years

10. Macedon's Conquest of Greece: The Rise of Philip II
11. Greece Attacks Persia: The Rise of Alexander the Great
12. Alexander Conquers the Persian Empire: From Egypt to India

title: Myth, Magic and Religion in the Ancient World
description: This course uses written and visual sources to examine public and private roles of religion, and explores the nature of myth in relation to society. As this is an advanced course in Ancient History, some knowledge of the Greek and Roman worlds would certainly help, but this does not preclude beginners. If you wish to discuss any aspect of this course, please contact the course coordinator. This course may not run if there are fewer than 20 enrolments. This second-year Ancient History course introduces students to the major myths, religious rituals, and magical practices of ancient Greece and Rome, and gives students the chance to read major works of Classical Greek and Roman mythological and religious literature in translation. This course helps students to become better ancient historians by developing their skills in critical thinking, source analysis, and the use of archaeological and literary evidence in thinking, talking and writing about Ancient History.
learning_outcomes: To understand the basic myths, religious rituals and magical practices of the ancient Greeks and Romans.
To use major works of Classical literature from ancient Greece and Rome as evidence for ancient mythology and religion, as well as political, social and cultural history.
To answer questions from ancient history by collecting relevant evidence and engaging with relevant modern scholarship.
To coordinate literary evidence and archaeological evidence in speaking and writing about Ancient History.
To work independently as a researcher and as part of different types of research teams.

5: Religious Practices and Practicing Religion

title: The Roman Republic
description: This course will examine certain important themes, problems and individuals associated with the history of the Roman Republic. The course may not run with 15 or fewer enrolments. This course offers in-depth study of the Roman republic during the second and first centuries BCE. A programme of linked lectures and tutorials explores key themes, problems, and scholarly approaches in Roman history within a broadly chronological framework. Themes include religion, politics, empire, and the intersections between them; also the position of Roman women, enslaved persons, and the built environment of Rome. Students will engage with some of the most important personalities and events of the period, including Cato the Elder, Cicero, and Caesar, the Catilinarian conspiracy, and the civil wars of the 40s BCE. Particular emphasis will be placed on analysis of contemporary ancient evidence, including Cicero's letters and speeches, and on comparing contemporary evidence with later sources.
learning_outcomes: demonstrate understanding of the operation of Roman government and society during the 2nd-1st centuries BCE
demonstrate understanding of important events and individuals in the period studied
critically analyse ancient sources such as texts, inscriptions, coins, and material culture
demonstrate familiarity with and engage with a range of modern perspectives and interpretations
carry out independent research, manage the information obtained, and communicate findings both verbally and in written form in a scholarly and coherent way

- A city of concrete

- The justice of empire

- Wars worse than civil
- Revision and exam preparation

title: War and Games in Democratic Athens
description: This course will examine a special topic in Ancient History determined by the research backgrounds of the teaching staff. It affords a special opportunity to experience research topics, methods and outcomes in Ancient History at an advanced level. ﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠﾠ This third-year course of 3 hours per week investigates the endlessly fascinating and complex relationship between war, games and democracy in ancient Athens. In addition to democratic politics, the overriding passions of the Athenian people were the waging of war and the staging of agōnes or games as part of religious festivals. By the time Athenian democracy was consolidated, war dominated the politics of this famous city and the lives of the dēmos or people. Athens of the fifth and fourth centuries BC waged wars almost constantly, was Greece’s premier innovator in military affairs and dominated the eastern Mediterranean militarily. This classical polis was famous for spending more time and money than any other city-state on state-sponsored festivals. Many Athenian religious celebrations had agōnes (‘contests’) in a wide variety of athletic, equestrian, musical or dramatic events and it was out of such games that the new genres of ancient theatre emerged.
learning_outcomes: Understand the salient features of, and the general interplay between, the war-making of the classical Athenians, their staging of games as part of religious festivals and the practices of their democracy
Employ the major classes of evidence from classical Athens for historical research
Identify some key controversies and problems in the historiography of classical Athens
Collect and analyse appropriate data in order to answer important research questions
Communicate the results of independent research as a research paper and an oral presentation in accordance with the disciplinary conventions of Ancient History
Understand the value of social-science theory and models for Humanities-based research
Work cooperatively as part of a research forum and a research team
Rehearse general skills that are required for graduate-level jobs in the public and private sectors

9. Honours for Panhellenic Sporting Victors

11. The Social Background of Athenian Sportsmen
12. Public Spending in Democratic Athens
13. Sport and War in Democratic Athens

title: Crime and the Courts in the Ancient World
description: This course takes an in-depth look at the workings of the law courts and some of the most important trials that took place in ancient Greece and Rome. The course spans archaic Greece to the high Roman empire, with particular attention to Classical Athens and the late Roman republic. Each lecture examines a different area of ancient law and its historical development, followed by close study of a particular trial speech in the linked tutorial. These texts include allegations of murder, treason, and magic, and high-powered oratory by two of the ancient world’s most famous speakers: Demosthenes and Cicero. In the process, the course will explore issues and themes that have helped to shape modern legal systems, including the use of trial by jury, the idea of equality before the law, and the relationship between law and politics. We recommend completing the following courses before enrolling in this one:
learning_outcomes: Identify the main features of a criminal trial in ancient Athens or Rome
Analyse ancient forensic oratory and legal problems (in English translation)
Discuss key individuals and trials of the period studied
Build arguments about the political, social, and historical significance of ancient law and trials
Understand and articulate the impact of ancient Greek and Roman law on our contemporary society
Conduct independent research and communicate findings in a scholarly manner
Communicate and interact effectively with teachers and peers
topics: 1: Introduction
2: Poets, laws, and gods
3: Violence and the law in 4th century Athens
4: Equality before the law?
5: Marriage and property in classical Athens
6: Oratory and deceiving the people
7: The Reception of Ancient Trials
8: Cicero, oratory, and the courts
9: Criminal courts in the Roman republic
10: Murder on the Appian Way Political violence in republican Rome
11: Plots, poisonings, and the princeps Trials in the early principate
12: Imperial justice Law and justice in the Roman empire
13: Reflections and revision

title: Systemic & Applied Anatomy
description: Integrated course in systematic gross anatomy with applications specific to further study in occupational therapy. Structures of the integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, endocrine, cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems will be examined. This systemic anatomy course provides students with an opportunity to develop an understanding of the interdependence of structure and function of human anatomy at the level of:
learning_outcomes: ANATOMICAL TERMINOLOGY: Communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology.
ANATOMICAL IDENTIFICATION: Identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of each of the major human organ systems on real human anatomical (cadaveric) specimens.
ANATOMICAL STRUCTURE: Describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute each major human organ system.
ANATOMICAL RELATIONSHIPS: Summarise the relationships between the various organ systems.
ANATOMICAL ORGAN FUNCTION: Define the principal function/s of each major human organ system.
FORM-FUNCTION INTERDEPENDENCE: Apply the concept of interdependent 'form and function' to illustrate how the structure of an organ system accomplishes its functions.
STRUCTURAL & FUNCTIONAL CHANGE: Demonstrate the ability to predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure.

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 1 - SKELETAL SYSTEM (Osteology & Arthrology) [Glen] WEEKS 1-4 This module introduces the student to the study of bones (osteology) and joints (arthrology), and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the skeletal system. As the skeleton forms the framework of the human body, this module also familiarises the student with general structure, arrangement and movements of the body. A detailed understanding of the structure and function of the bones and joints, and organisation of the skeletal system is particularly important foundational knowledge for learning the muscular system in the next module, since the muscles are attached to these skeletal structures and drive their movements about these joints

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 2 - MUSCULAR SYSTEM (Myology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of muscles (myology), and comprehensively examines the structure and function most of the of the tissues, organs and anatomy of the muscular system. As the muscular system drives and coordinates the movement and orientation of the body, and assists the skeleton in support and protection, this module also further familiarises the student with the general structure, arrangement and movements of the body, as well as develops an understanding of how these movements are caused and coordinated. A detailed knowledge of the attachment and arrangement of the muscles relative to the bones and joints of the skeleton (covered in the previous module) is important foundational knowledge for understanding how muscles and groups of muscles generate and coordinate movement and orientation of the body

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 3 - NERVOUS & ENDOCRINE SYSTEMS (Neurology & Endocrinology) [Aland] This module introduces the student to the study of the nervous system (neurology) and endocrine system (endocrinology) from an anatomical perspective, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these two systems. Students will learn that both systems are involved in sending signals from one part of the body to another (and also interact with each other): the nervous system can send extremely fast and precisely targeted electrochemical signals from one end of the body to the other; whereas the endocrine system generally broadcasts chemical signals through fluids of the body that trigger responses in tissues sensitive to them. This knowledge is foundational for the understanding of how the anatomy of the nervous system' relates to its role in the coordination and processing of sensory input, coordination of movement of the musculoskeletal system, and its signalling to key physiological rates and states; and understanding how the anatomical arrangement of endocrine organs relate to their function

learning_activities.topic: MODULE 4 - VISCERAL SYSTEMS (Splanchnology) [Glen] This module introduces the student to the study of the visceral systems (splanchnology) which includes the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems, and examines the structure and function of the tissues, organs and anatomy of these. "Viscera" refers to the soft internal organs housed centrally in the body, but note that the cardiovascular and lymphatic systems include vessels that branch out into the extremities. The student will learn how the anatomy of these organs relate to their function

ANATOMICAL IDENTIFICATION: Identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of each of the major human organ systems on real human anatomical (cadaveric) specimens. ANATOMICAL STRUCTURE: Describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute each major human organ system. ANATOMICAL RELATIONSHIPS: Summarise the relationships between the various organ systems. ANATOMICAL ORGAN FUNCTION: Define the principal function/s of each major human organ system. FORM-FUNCTION INTERDEPENDENCE: Apply the concept of interdependent 'form and function' to illustrate how the structure of an organ system accomplishes its functions. STRUCTURAL & FUNCTIONAL CHANGE: Demonstrate the ability to predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure.

title: Being Human: Cultural Diversity and Experience

assessments.task: Participating in the tutorials & online workshops  Online
Doing Anthropology - Final Essay and Coded Notes  Online

description: Anthropologists are experts in the 'how' of human diversity and anthropological insights into the infinite range of human experiences matter for understanding the world today. This course provides an introduction to anthropological approaches to knowing, focussing on questions of how humans make society and culture, and why this matters. Students are introduced to the diverse components of anthropology and also get to do what anthropologists do: practicing our hallmark methods of fieldwork and participant observation, and learning how to analyse and write about the fieldwork data. Specific topics in the discipline are practised by students through undertaking research 'at home'. As an introductory course in the discipline of anthropology, we ask, how does 'culture' matter in human history, for economic relations and environments? How do gender, religion, art, economics, politics, inequality, and human rights have diverse cultural dimensions and implications? Case material students read and refer to explores the social dynamics of globalisation and capitalism and their others in different reaches of the planet and we consider the application of anthropological knowledge in development, health and scientific endeavours.
learning_outcomes: A well-founded understanding of anthropology, in particular how it relates to and differs from other disciplines and contributes to understanding the diversity of human worlds.
The ability to apply ethically informed and critical analysis in responses to contemporary problems and in the interests of fairness, equality and inclusivity.
The skills to collect, organise and analyse sociocultural research including that drawing from the anthropological literature and that resulting from original research.
Proficiency and fluency in oral and written communication that conveys knowledge, ideas and complex questions about social relationships and human culture.
topics: Introduction & short history of anthropology Welcome to anthropology! The first lecture will provide a short history of the discipline of anthropology and raise a series of key questions that the course seeks to address. We will also go over the course content, assessment and other details of what to expect. Prepare for the lecture by reading over all the course materials and then read the first chapter of the textbook, Eller (2015) chapter 1, as well as the article by Proctor (2022). What are your questions about anthropology? Write a list of all the things you want to know and ask the best ones during class

Human Diversity & ways to think it Anthropology is unendingly interested in human societies, culture and all the diverse ways in which humans express ourselves as social (and sometimes anti-social!) beings, each one unique and with so many things in common. Enter the world of contradiction - how are humans one species and yet so diverse? What are the implications of this diversity and some of the history of understanding it? To prepare for the lecture, read the textbook, Eller Chapter 2, and the article by Graeber & Wengrow (2021). You might have additional time to consider the recommended reading (a chapter of a book by Anne Salmond). TUTORIAL: To prepare, write a short summary in your own words of the article by Graeber & Wengrow (2021). This is not assessed, but it will be useful for participating in the tutorial. In class, we will discuss how (and why) you should learn to write a good article summary, but give it a go on your own before class

Culture, change, and globalisation In the lecture we will examine the concept of 'culture', consider the ways that anthropologists trace cultural continuities and changes and think about culture in the context of globalisation. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 3 and the article by Li & Manzano (2021). The recommended reading by Mintz (1986, chapter 1) is a classic on the history of sugar and globalisation. TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes) to help guide your questions in class

Relations: Kinship, the body and society In the lecture we will examine how anthropologists consider 'relations' and what we mean by the term 'kinship'. Students will learn the basics of 'mapping' kinship and consider how our human bodies are formed as social and cultural processes. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 4 and the article by Jiang (2024). The recommended reading by Janice Boddy (2016) deals with (for some) confronting aspects of culturally legitimated body modification - **Trigger warning** please read with caution! TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes)

Communication, translation & representation How is culture social (not individual)? Culture and social meanings are shared through different forms of communication. How do we think across cultures, or translate from one to the other? What are the problems of speaking and writing about (representing) cultures, both our own and others? Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 5 and the article by Blasi (2024). Consider watching the entire film "Arrival" and reading the recommended readings as well. Muehlmann (2008) makes an argument for the special place of swear-words (" groserias ") in one Indigenous language (northern Mexico) - dive in! TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes)
In class exam This mid-semester exam will be run in the lecture time. The exam will be held in-person only. Please see assessment for all details

Value and moralities: anthropological approaches to economics In the lecture we will examine how anthropologists consider economics to be cultural and social practice, revealing of value, values and moralities. We examine the history of money, the social processes in value creation and different experiences and meanings attributed to labour. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 6, and the book chapter by Graeber (2014), and perhaps also the article by Heuts & Mol (2013) on value. TUTORIAL: Let's talk about economic value, other forms of value and moral life - bring your questions (based in the reading material) to class

Doing and thinking ethnography : lecture instruction The principle methodology of anthropology is ethnography, and it is sometimes also spoken of as a method, or a 'way of seeing'. How does ethnographic research work? In preparation for the lecture read the required readings, including the chapter from Eller (ch 9) and the Chapter by Lassiter (chapter 3). Consider reading chapter 7 of the book by Paige West on coffee. Bring your questions to the class. Instructions for the Online Methods Workshops will be provided during the lecture. TUTORIAL: Prepare for the Tutorial by reading the Eller chapter (9) and Lassiter (chapter 3) and begin online method workshops (Workshop one, notes, and upload to padlet.)

Colonial politics & the state This lecture opens up a discussion of the anthropology of politics. We will return to colonial and globalising dynamics, and give some background to what anthropologists might mean when we use the word 'politics'. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 7 and the article by Stead (2019) and recommended reading by Anderson (2003) or Kirsch (2009). How do anthropologists study politics and how is this different to other social scientists' approaches? TUTORIAL: Bring your notes and questions on the topic discussion, from reading Eller, Chapter 7 and Stead. Do the Online Method Workshop 2 on Kinship

Ritual, religion and the symbolic What IS religion? What does ritual DO? The lecture will consider a range of questions that anthropologists of religion and ritual have asked in order to contribute to understandings of human diversity. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 8 and the book chapter by Bielo (2015) and bring your notes to class. Consider the required readings as well. NO Face to Face TUTORIAL. Do online method workshop 3, and upload your results of your observation to the padlet

Ecology, energy, and connections How do environmental anthropologists contribute to useful thinking on ecological problems? What are the connections that make ecological worlds social and cultural? How do new questions about energy matter to anthropologists? FILM "The Anthropologist" introduces some ways to think about environments and ethnographic work. Prepare for the lecture by reading the article by Begert (2021) and the chapter by Halvksz (2021). Consider the recommended reading by Henning (2005). TUTORIAL: Bring your notes and questions on the topic discussion, informed by required & recommended reading. Do the Online Methods Workshop 4 on Interviewing

Essay Writing and Analysis : instructional lecture The lecture will provide detailed instructions for the final essay. Students are encouraged to come to class having finalised the research steps toward their essay draft before the lecture (that is, having done their interview and some literature searches) and to bring their informed questions about next steps to the class. TUTORIAL: The tutorial will also focus on the research process and encourage students to use the space to finalise the online workshops and raise any questions about these assessments

Where do we apply anthropology? Examples from 'Development', Design and Health applications The final lecture will consider what you can do with an education in anthropology. What questions do we ask and how do we put our questions to work ? Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 10 and the book chapter by Gardener & Lewis (2015)

learning_activities.topic: Introduction & short history of anthropology Welcome to anthropology! The first lecture will provide a short history of the discipline of anthropology and raise a series of key questions that the course seeks to address. We will also go over the course content, assessment and other details of what to expect. Prepare for the lecture by reading over all the course materials and then read the first chapter of the textbook, Eller (2015) chapter 1, as well as the article by Proctor (2022). What are your questions about anthropology? Write a list of all the things you want to know and ask the best ones during class

Human Diversity & ways to think it Anthropology is unendingly interested in human societies, culture and all the diverse ways in which humans express ourselves as social (and sometimes anti-social!) beings, each one unique and with so many things in common. Enter the world of contradiction - how are humans one species and yet so diverse? What are the implications of this diversity and some of the history of understanding it? To prepare for the lecture, read the textbook, Eller Chapter 2, and the article by Graeber & Wengrow (2021). You might have additional time to consider the recommended reading (a chapter of a book by Anne Salmond). TUTORIAL: To prepare, write a short summary in your own words of the article by Graeber & Wengrow (2021). This is not assessed, but it will be useful for participating in the tutorial. In class, we will discuss how (and why) you should learn to write a good article summary, but give it a go on your own before class

Culture, change, and globalisation In the lecture we will examine the concept of 'culture', consider the ways that anthropologists trace cultural continuities and changes and think about culture in the context of globalisation. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 3 and the article by Li & Manzano (2021). The recommended reading by Mintz (1986, chapter 1) is a classic on the history of sugar and globalisation. TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes) to help guide your questions in class

Relations: Kinship, the body and society In the lecture we will examine how anthropologists consider 'relations' and what we mean by the term 'kinship'. Students will learn the basics of 'mapping' kinship and consider how our human bodies are formed as social and cultural processes. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 4 and the article by Jiang (2024). The recommended reading by Janice Boddy (2016) deals with (for some) confronting aspects of culturally legitimated body modification - **Trigger warning** please read with caution! TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes)

Communication, translation & representation How is culture social (not individual)? Culture and social meanings are shared through different forms of communication. How do we think across cultures, or translate from one to the other? What are the problems of speaking and writing about (representing) cultures, both our own and others? Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 5 and the article by Blasi (2024). Consider watching the entire film "Arrival" and reading the recommended readings as well. Muehlmann (2008) makes an argument for the special place of swear-words (" groserias ") in one Indigenous language (northern Mexico) - dive in! TUTORIAL: Bring a copy of your notes and summary of reading material to the tutorial to help you participate in the topic discussion. A discussion question will be raised in the lecture (and available in the lecture notes)
In class exam This mid-semester exam will be run in the lecture time. The exam will be held in-person only. Please see assessment for all details

Value and moralities: anthropological approaches to economics In the lecture we will examine how anthropologists consider economics to be cultural and social practice, revealing of value, values and moralities. We examine the history of money, the social processes in value creation and different experiences and meanings attributed to labour. Prepare for the lecture by reading Eller Chapter 6, and the book chapter by Graeber (2014), and perhaps also the article by Heuts & Mol (2013) on value. TUTORIAL: Let's talk about economic value, other forms of value and moral life - bring your questions (based in the reading material) to class

search_blob: ANTH1008 Being Human: Cultural Diversity and Experience | Anthropologists are experts in the 'how' of human diversity and anthropological insights into the infinite range of human experiences matter for understanding the world today. This course provides an introduction to anthropological approaches to knowing, focussing on questions of how humans make society and culture, and why this matters. Students are introduced to the diverse components of anthropology and also get to do what anthropologists do: practicing our hallmark methods of fieldwork and participant observation, and learning how to analyse and write about the fieldwork data. Specific topics in the discipline are practised by students through undertaking research 'at home'. As an introductory course in the discipline of anthropology, we ask, how does 'culture' matter in human history, for economic relations and environments? How do gender, religion, art, economics, politics, inequality, and human rights have diverse cultural dimensions and implications? Case material students read and refer to explores the social dynamics of globalisation and capitalism and their others in different reaches of the planet and we consider the application of anthropological knowledge in development, health and scientific endeavours. | Introduction & short history of anthropology Welcome to anthropology! The ability to apply ethically informed and critical analysis in responses to contemporary problems and in the interests of fairness, equality and inclusivity. The skills to collect, organise and analyse sociocultural research including that drawing from the anthropological literature and that resulting from original research. Proficiency and fluency in oral and written communication that conveys knowledge, ideas and complex questions about social relationships and human culture.

title: Ecology, Culture, and Field Research

assessments.task: In-class research presentation and written reflection
Group research team work: Peer assessment

description: In much of traditional Western thinking, nature and culture have generally been treated as separate. Based on fieldwork in communities around the world, anthropologists were among the first social scientists to change this view and to consider nature and culture together. Students in this course explore issues relating to the way people know, interact with, and care about their environment. This includes human interactions with, and the meanings of, (urban) wildlife, climate change studies, biodiversity conservation, bush fire management, political ecology, and the societal questionsﾠsurroundingﾠresource developments. In addition, students will develop skills in one of the hallmarks of anthropological enquiry: ethnographic fieldwork. The field of Environmental Anthropology now draws on insights from the social sciences, as well as disciplines such as ecology, biology, natural resource management, politics and geography. This course will explain some of the major research areas in environmental anthropology today.ﾠStudents will learn how to set up a small research project related to the Brisbane environment, and will then complete related fieldwork research training.ﾠFinally, students will use the empirical data collected during the fieldwork research training to write a small essay (2,000 words). Students will therefore not only learn about contemporary research areasﾠand get to exploreﾠsome of the relationships between humans and the environment in Brisbane and beyond, the course will also include vital development of field research skills, data analysis, writing approaches and presentation of results.
learning_outcomes: Communicate effectively on the main topics raised in the course;
Have an understanding of environmental anthropology and how it can be used as a tool to critically explore issues involving human-environment relations;
To understand how and why global and local processes merge to affect local actors interactions with the environment, including their political, sociocultural and economic dimensions;

To critically question broader assumptions and representations of nature and society and, in particular, how these understandings inform environmental management, disputes, climate change and political debates.
To understand and appreciate human-environment relations, and how these relations and concomitant meanings shape local struggles in important and sometimes inequitable ways.

Undertake research as part of a group. This includes professional teamwork, commitment to group goals, effective communication and respectful discussion with team members about research plans, execution, results and analysis.

learning_activities.topic: Module I Plants and Animals LECTURE: Nature, Culture, Non-human Animals: An introduction to key concepts. TUTORIAL: Group research training, expectations, responsibilities and available support. Given the focus on various research topics as well as research training in environmental anthropology, this course requires a high degree of professionalism among the students. Some tasks need to be completed as part of a group, and this requires attendance, commitment and support from all those involved. Should there be complaints or unresolvable conflict among members of a group, the problem should be brought to the attention of the course coordinator for early intervention and resolution
Module I Plants and Animals LECTURE: Wild urban companions, and connections between people and nature. TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion

Module I Plants and Animals LECTURE: Human-wildlife interactions in urban environments. Guest presentation by Prof Darryl Jones, Ecologist, Griffith University. TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion
Module I Plants and Animals: Research Preparation LECTURE: Case study and field research training TUTORIAL: Q&A about research plan development, including methods, ethics and research participants
Module II Subsurface Environments LECTURE: Mythologies of the Underground TUTORIAL: In-class activity that addresses: Are chthonic imaginaries relevant today?
No class (Good Friday)
Mid-semester break No class
Module II Subsurface Environments LECTURE: Art and political economy of the underground TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion
Module III Fire, Energy and Culture LECTURE: The anthropology of (bush) fire and energy part I TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion
Module III Fire, Energy and Culture LECTURE: The anthropology of (bush) fire and energy part II TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion
Module IV Living in the Anthropocene LECTURE: Resource materialities and the Anthropocene TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class discussion
Module IV Living in the Anthropocene LECTURE: Climate, Country & Caring TUTORIAL: Group research and in-class preparation for the week 12 assessment

In-class presentations and feedback Each group presents the preliminary results of the group research project and provides written feedback on the presentation of the other groups. Further details to be provided on Blackboard
Course Re-Cap and Essay Preparation Human-environment relations: what did we learn and how to write it up?
