title: Learning, Using and Sharing Indigenous Languages

assessments.task: Rationale and Reflection - Language Learning Plan  Online
Group Article Review Team or group-based Online

description: Sharing and learning Indigenous languages occurs in a range of contexts, with learners spanning infants to adults, and settings ranging from creek beds to classrooms to kitchen tables. For many Indigenous learners, it involves reconnection with identity and ancestors, revisiting pain and beginning healing. In this course, we explore these settings, goals and meanings for language learning, and the shared and differing demands they pose for language teachers and learners. Drawing on a growing national and international practitioner literature, this course supports students to develop mastery in planning and evaluating learning programs most suited to specific their context, including independent language learning.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate understanding of the diversity of Indigenous language learning contexts.
Understand the impacts of colonisation on Indigenous languages loss and the activism to maintain and revitalize them.
Articulate protocols, and ethical practices associated with teaching, learning and using Indigenous languages.

Effectively plan and develop a unit within a language program or a learning plan, tailored to the needs of diverse learners, incorporating best practices from First Nations language teaching methodologies and contemporary second language acquisition strategies.
Demonstrate high level communication skills including structuring and supporting an argument.

learning_activities.topic: Intensive Day 1 Session 1 Diversity of Indigenous language learning contexts Diversity of goals for Language learning and Language revitalisation Impacts of colonisation on Indigenous languages Session 2 How do we learn sounds? stages of first language (L1) acquisition – how babies learn the sounds around them differences between first language (L1) learning and second language (L2) learning strategies for learners and 'unlearners' Session 3 Active language learning What is the place of accent, fluency, proficiency, accreditation in Indigenous Language revitalisation? Review of strategies for language learning Setting goals for language learning Assignment planning – Task 3 Journal

Intensive Day 2 Session 1 How are languages learnt? stages of first language (L1) acquisition – how babies learn the language/s around them formulaic language – repeating familiar phrases correctly ‘interlanguage’ or ‘learner language’ differences between first language (L1) learning and second language (L2) learning Factors affecting second language (L2) acquisition (part 1) Session 2 How are languages learnt? Factors affecting second language (L2) acquisition (part 1) Age First language (L1) background (e.g. difficulties with sounds and sound combinations, transfer of meanings, difference or similarity of grammar) Prior language experiences and explicit language learning Prior literacy experiences (reading, writing, comprehension) Session 3 Factors affecting second language (L2) acquisition (part 3) Comprehensible input hypothesis Sequencing language learning – an exemplar Assignment planning - Task 1 Language learning plan

Intensive Day 3 Session 1 Introduction to language teaching approaches through hands-on activities Behaviourist: For beginner learners, some rote-learning is useful (learners tend to do this strategically in the early stages of acquisition anyway) Structuralist: Explicitly teaching language structures (i.e, generalisations) at an appropriate level assists students’ acquisition of these structures Functional: Teaching situational language (i.e, language for specific purposes) is a useful way to target teaching and provide language that is useful for students Interactive: Using language (if you have it) is fun – language has a fundamentally social function. Using and practising language is essential for developing fluency. Language teaching approaches – Behaviourist Rote learning, Drilling and repetition, Songs and rhymes TPR – Total Physical Response, ASLA – Accelerated Second Language Acquisition Opportunities for rote language learning through digital technologies Session 2 Language teaching approaches – Structured/Explicit Explicit grammar teaching Translation activities Fill-in-the-blanks Error correction Opportunities for structured/explicit language learning through digital technologies Session 3 Language teaching approaches – Functional Task-based language for specific purposes, contexts and domains (Amery – the four Fs?) Modelling and scaffolding Role-play scenarios Opportunities for functional language learning through digital technologies Assignment planning – Task 1 & 4

Intensive Day 4 Session 1 Language teaching approaches – Interactive/Immersive Implicit learning Communication-centred Group learning Master-apprentice ‘Immersion’ language learning in a language revitalisation context Opportunities for interactive/immersive language learning through digital technologies Session 2 Age-appropriate pedagogies Repurposing resources and activities across age groups Session 3 Planning for Assessment – Task 4 Group reading review

Intensive Day 5 Session 1 Protocols and ethical practices associated with teaching and learning Indigenous languages Indigenous Cultural and Intellectual Property (ICIP) Involvement of Elders Communication and agreements between language speakers/owners and education providers Protocols and ethics of being on country Bringing it together - Planning for language teaching and learning Constraints of the teaching and learning environment Resources available How to plan a sequence of lessons that build on each other How to give feedback to learners Session 2 Bringing grammatical knowledge into language learning through plain language descriptions Guest talk from Gari Tudor Smith Session 3 Recap, review and assessments
Post-Workshop Online session 1 Recap and review - Planning for language teaching and learning Review Assessment 1
Post-Workshop Online session 2 Critical reading - literature on learning for group review Focus on Assessment 3
Post-Workshop Online session 3 Share and reflect on assessment 2 video submissions
Post-Workshop Online session 4 Guest Speaker to be announced
Post-Workshop Online session 5 Links to AUIL7203 Project planning

title: Building Strong Language Communities

assessments.task: Review - Incorporating Feedback Into Your Proposal Online

description: Indigenous Language revitalisation champions build strong language communities through diverse language and cultural activities, growing language in use and making language more visible in public life. In this course, we examine ways that language planning, policy and status locally and globally open up or close down spaces for languages. Students consolidate and apply knowledge and practical skills developed throughout the program and extend project design and management skills by creating a community-focussed language project.
learning_outcomes: Explain connections between language policy, prestige, visibility and action.
Define a problem or need for a user group or audience in a Language Revitalisation context.
Scope resources, networks and opportunities, identify and manage risks, design strategies to meet project needs.

Review and evaluate strategies for a Language Revitalisation initiative using a range of tools and approaches, including peer and Community feedback, and reference to local and wider policy and goals.

learning_activities.topic: Online Session Language Policy & Revitalisation in Action o Guest speaker – Clayton Cruse. Title tba o Discussion of Planning materials & activities for intensive

Online Session 1. Intro Technology for Language work What technologies do you/your community currently use in ILR? What technologies do you want to use? title: Contemporary Australia

assessments.task: Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale Online

description: This course explores key issues and debates in understanding Australia as a nation, a culture and a society. Topics examine the forces shaping contemporary Australia and their historical context. The course is designed for both local and international students. AUST1000 is an interdisciplinary course, just as Australian Studies is an interdisciplinary field. Students will be introduced to recent developments in the fields of media/cultural studies, literature, history, politics, Indigenous studies and gender studies. A range of cultural texts including film, television, print media and advertisements will be used to develop the key concepts of the course. This course develops a range of skills valued by employers. You will develop knowledge, attributes, skills, and experience in the following:
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of the central issues affecting contemporary understandings of Australia as a nation
Be able to compare, contrast, and analyse a range of different perspectives on current debates about Australian society and culture
Be able to make links between individual identities and national identities
Show reasonable knowledge of the historical context of current issues and recognise ways in which history is re-interpreted to meet contemporary needs
Demonstrate an understanding of concepts and methods important to the field of Australian Studies

learning_activities.topic: : Introduction This week we will go through important information related to the course including Blackboard, assessments, learning approaches and what to expect. We will also begin looking at key critical issues and debates that have shaped, and continue to influence Australia today

: Visual Thinking Strategies This week we will begin with a Visual Thinking Strategy Session, presented by the UQ Art Museum. This session will be vital to the development of critical thinking skills needed for this course as well your learning journey more broadly. VTS teaches learners to carefully observe, evaluate, synthesise, justify and speculate – all essential aspects for critical thinking. It has been measured as an effective tool to build visual thinking skills, which directly benefits graduate performance, including exam results

: Australian National Identity: Mythology, Power, and Privilege This week we will critically examine some prominent myths about Australian national identity such as the myth of Terra Nullius, the myth of Egalitarianism and ‘a fair go’, and the ANZAC myth. We will ask questions such as: Whose interests do these mythologies serve? Who is included and who is rendered invisible? What do these mythologies tell us about Australia politically, socially, and culturally?

: Indigenous Identities: Activism and Resistance This week we will explore historical events that have shaped First Nations Peoples. We will look at government policies that have, and continue to, impacted First Nations peoples. We will also explore acts of resistance and activism by First Nation people from Australia’s first wars, the establishment of the Aboriginal Tent Embassy in Canberra, Nicky Winmar’s stand against racial discrimination, and more

: Multicultural Identities in Australia The first migrants to Colonial Australia were prisoners of the British Crown who were transported to the country as punishment. Since then, there have been different waves of immigration that have established Australia as a multicultural nation - an identity that has been subject to both celebration and controversy. This week we will track historical attitudes, policies, and practices that have informed cultural ideas about immigration and national identity

: Gender and sexuality in Contemporary Australia This week we will look at hegemony and how tropes and ideologies about gender operate to maintain hegemonic power structures. However, our main focus will be to explore diverse, historically overlooked gender identities in Australian society
: Fryer Library Excursion This week we will be going to the Fryer Library. This excursion will be valuable for your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale

: Refugees and Asylum Seekers: Policies and Practice As we saw in Week 5, Colonial Australia has history of discrimination when it comes to the country’s borders and who it will allow in, dating back to the implementation of the Immigration Restriction Act , in 1901. Since the mid-1980s Australian governments have increasingly implemented hard lined immigration policies that has led the UN to warn that Australia's mandatory detention regime for refugees and asylum seekers is violating international human rights. This week we will examine some of the issues facing refugees and asylum seekers

: Autographic Practice: Agency, Advocacy, and Representation This week we will look at the way asylum seekers, First Nations, and culturally/gender diverse people use autobiographical comics to voice issues of subjectivity, embodiment, and belonging. This week’s topic is designed to inspire you and get you thinking creatively about your digital exhibition concept and rationale
PUBLIC HOLIDAY: NO SEMINARS THIS WEEK

: Contemporary Australia and Counternarratives on Social Media This week we will continue our exploration of alternative modes of contemporary storytelling by looking at the way Australian film makers and artists are using Social Media platforms to share alternative, culturally diverse narratives

: Contemporary Australia in Focus: Truth Telling and Treaty, Why it Matters With the first treaty between Indigenous people and the Australian government recently passed in Victoria law recently, this is an opportune time to discuss why treaty is significant, what truth telling is, and why it is essential to reconciliation. We will also spend some time discussing your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale
: Independent Study Week There will be no seminars this week. Instead it will be a time for you to finalise your Digital Exhibition Concept and Rationale

title: Plants and Archaeology: Food, fuel, foraging and farming in the human past

assessments.task: ARCS2080 Report: Archaeobotany methodology statement and recommendation Online

description: Whether as food, fuel or shelter, plants are among the fundamental building blocks of human life, and archaeology is increasingly able to reconstruct the global, deep-time history of human plant use from the evolution of our earliest ancestors. In this course, students will explore the entangled history of plants and people, including the role of plant foods and fire in human evolution, the global origins and development of farming, the central role of plants in trade and globalisation, and the history of landscape management, biodiversity loss and the emergence of the Anthropocene. Practical classes will introduce students to the methods of archaeobotany, the science that underpins these discoveries, including how plant remains can be routinely recovered from archaeological sites. Our course concludes with a consideration of how archaeological knowledge of the past is helping to revive cultures, improve economic resilience and rebuild landscapes in the present. Plants have been a fundamentally important resource for human societies throughout our history and archaeobotany (also known as paleoethnobotany) provides a powerful means of understanding plant use and its contribution to human culture change. In this course we will provide an overview of human plant use and the means by which that history has been developed over the sequence of lectures and practical classes.
learning_outcomes: Understand the general principles, methods and theory of archaeobotany
Apply the analytical and theoretical approaches commonly used in archaeobotany to address research questions and understand human-plant interaction in the past
Demonstrate critical understanding of the major methodological issues in archaeobotany and their impact on interpretation
Understand the broader significance of archaeobotanical data to major archaeological and transdisciplinary questions and their appropriate application in these contexts
topics: Why study plants in archaeology? ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This module introduces the scope, origins, history and importance of archaeobotany, providing the intellectual foundation for the course. Watch before class in Week 1

Plants as food: Preservation and identification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant foods are a vital part of life, but hard to study in the past as their physical remains often are not preserved very well. After introducing what foods are, week explore how we can reconstruct the history of plant foods using archaeobotanical tools and a range of artefact and scientific approaches. As well as considering the variety of evidence, we consider how formation processes affect which foods we find, but also how taphonomy - the study of preservation - allows us to predict what we will find in specific environments and sites, empowering us to reconstruct food histories

Plant foods and human evolution ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we dig deep to explore how plant foods shaped human evolution, including the development of Homo sapiens and our huge brains. Forget all that nonsense about 'man the hunter' and find out how plant-based nutrition has been a part of our lives for millennia and was essential in making us human

Altered states: plants as medicines & intoxicants ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: When did medicines and intoxicants such as drugs and alcohol appear in the human career, how do we know and what effect did they have on human culture change? Simple questions, but ones that have challenged archaeobotanists to develop new techniques and radically change our understanding of an important aspect of human life

Plant trade, exchange and ancient globalisation ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plants have been widely traded for millennia and that trade drove significant cultural, economic and social transformations in the deep past. In this lecture we look at how archaeology can reconstruct plant trade and some key studies of its history from the origins of agriculture and sedentism, to the origins of states, globalisation and European colonialism

Fuels and landscape management ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: As the main source of fuels for heat, cooking and industry, woodland and forests have been a major focus for landscape modification and management, and we look at how fuel histories can be constructed and what they tell us in a story now extending into the Pleistocene

Plants in ancient architecture, crafts and industry ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant products, including wood, fibres, waxes and stems have been used to produce everything from buildings and artefacts to clothing and ornaments. This week we survey the history of plants beyond food and intoxicants from the Pleistocene to modern world

Gathering for a living ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we start our exploration into the diverse ways humans have sustained themselves. We begin with foraging, also known as hunting and gathering, in which plants are harvested from natural vegetation growth. Sustaining humans since we first evolved, foraging has a deep and diverse history and with ever better methods we have increasingly detailed data from archaeological sites. Food is, however, not just about nutrition, and we also explore how food acquisition and sharing has developed as a key element of social behaviour

Agriculture, cultivation and domestication ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture is the production of food and other resources from plants (and animals) that are managed and domesticated - meaning they are under human control and selection - and the world's vast population and complex global society is built on it. This week we consider the diversity of agricultural systems and the key concepts cultivation and domestication

Global perspectives on the origins of agriculture ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Today's global society is built on the ancient and diverse foundations of ancient farming systems. This week we explore how archaeobotany has contributed to explaining where, when and why farming started at diverse places around the world in the deep past

Agriculture's ancient diversification and intensification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture has undergone significant changes as it spread and developed around the world. This week we look at some important moments of transformation, including the development of mixed farming, intensification, the secondary products revolution and the effects of trade on farming systems in some of the world's earliest examples of globalisation

Was Australia really a farming bystander? Evaluating Bruce Pascoe's "Dark Emu" ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we look into the issue of how Australia's first people's exploited and shaped the landscape of this continent, including discussing whether they ever developed farming as proposed by Bruce Pascoe in his controversial work "Dark Emu"

Archaeobotany & the challenges of the Anthropocene ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: An understanding of past plant use and landscapes from archaeology is increasingly being used to revitalise and improve resilience of cultures, economies and landscapes today in the face of an unpredictable future. We conclude our course by exploring the potential and future of archaeobotany in the wider world

learning_activities.topic: Why study plants in archaeology? ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This module introduces the scope, origins, history and importance of archaeobotany, providing the intellectual foundation for the course. Watch before class in Week 1

Welcome to Plants and Archaeology This foundation lab session introduces the course, its aims, assessment and structure, building on the online module to explore why you should care about plants and the human past as archaeologists in training. Includes workshop activities on "what are plants?" and "why are plants important?" and a class Q&A to resolve any uncertainties about the course. We also introduce our study area for Assessment 2, which you will prepare for during the whole course

Plants as food: Preservation and identification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant foods are a vital part of life, but hard to study in the past as their physical remains often are not preserved very well. After introducing what foods are, week explore how we can reconstruct the history of plant foods using archaeobotanical tools and a range of artefact and scientific approaches. As well as considering the variety of evidence, we consider how formation processes affect which foods we find, but also how taphonomy - the study of preservation - allows us to predict what we will find in specific environments and sites, empowering us to reconstruct food histories

Module A: The diversity of plant remains In class we explore the types of plant remains found in archaeological sites, macrofossils and mincrofossils, and find out when and why they are preserved. This session introduces the main microscopy methods used in the practical classes and also the scientific standards expected in our work

Plant foods and human evolution ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we dig deep to explore how plant foods shaped human evolution, including the development of Homo sapiens and our huge brains. Forget all that nonsense about 'man the hunter' and find out how plant-based nutrition has been a part of our lives for millennia and was essential in making us human

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 1: The wonderful world of seeds! This week we explore the wonders of seeds, the most commonly analysed and significant plant macrofossils in archaeobotany. We will look at how they are identified, including understanding the significance of landmarks and standard orientations in our work, and consider some of the challenges of quantification

Altered states: plants as medicines & intoxicants ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: When did medicines and intoxicants such as drugs and alcohol appear in the human career, how do we know and what effect did they have on human culture change? Simple questions, but ones that have challenged archaeobotanists to develop new techniques and radically change our understanding of an important aspect of human life

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 2: Crop remains in archaeobotany Crop remains are fundamentally important for understanding ancient economies and the history of agriculture across the world. We look into the seeds, chaff, straw and other elements that are preserved and discuss how assemblage analysis has transformed our understanding of past farming systems

Plant trade, exchange and ancient globalisation ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plants have been widely traded for millennia and that trade drove significant cultural, economic and social transformations in the deep past. In this lecture we look at how archaeology can reconstruct plant trade and some key studies of its history from the origins of agriculture and sedentism, to the origins of states, globalisation and European colonialism

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 3: Fruits and nuts This week we look at the diversity of fruit, nut and wild seed structures we find in archaeology and explore further how we can use measurements and simple statistics to understand their identity and use

Fuels and landscape management ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: As the main source of fuels for heat, cooking and industry, woodland and forests have been a major focus for landscape modification and management, and we look at how fuel histories can be constructed and what they tell us in a story now extending into the Pleistocene

Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 4: Indeterminate plant remains (wood and USO's) Indeterminate plant remains are fragments of larger plant structures, such as woody stems, that lack clear morphology and can't be resolved into whole items, as is the case with seeds. This week introduces the largely anatomical approaches we use to identify them, including wood charcoals and underground storage organs (think potatoes). Using wood charcoals as an example we also explore how to analyse indeterminate remains to reconstruct plant economies and landscape change

Plants in ancient architecture, crafts and industry ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant products, including wood, fibres, waxes and stems have been used to produce everything from buildings and artefacts to clothing and ornaments. This week we survey the history of plants beyond food and intoxicants from the Pleistocene to modern world
Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 1:

Gathering for a living ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we start our exploration into the diverse ways humans have sustained themselves. We begin with foraging, also known as hunting and gathering, in which plants are harvested from natural vegetation growth. Sustaining humans since we first evolved, foraging has a deep and diverse history and with ever better methods we have increasingly detailed data from archaeological sites. Food is, however, not just about nutrition, and we also explore how food acquisition and sharing has developed as a key element of social behaviour
Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 2:

Agriculture, cultivation and domestication ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture is the production of food and other resources from plants (and animals) that are managed and domesticated - meaning they are under human control and selection - and the world's vast population and complex global society is built on it. This week we consider the diversity of agricultural systems and the key concepts cultivation and domestication

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 1: Manual/bucket flotation At UQ's ATARC, we will explore how to extract plant remains via flotation. We begin by considering the aims of such recovery, its logistics and design, learning how to float samples using bucket flotation! This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear

Global perspectives on the origins of agriculture ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Today's global society is built on the ancient and diverse foundations of ancient farming systems. This week we explore how archaeobotany has contributed to explaining where, when and why farming started at diverse places around the world in the deep past

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 2: Machine and hand pump flotation At UQ's ATARC we explore the wonders of machine and hand pump flotation, which allow larger scale processing of soil samples. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear

Agriculture's ancient diversification and intensification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture has undergone significant changes as it spread and developed around the world. This week we look at some important moments of transformation, including the development of mixed farming, intensification, the secondary products revolution and the effects of trade on farming systems in some of the world's earliest examples of globalisation

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 3: Machine and hand pump flotation (continued) We continue and conclude our exploration of machine and hand pump flotation. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear

Was Australia really a farming bystander? Evaluating Bruce Pascoe's "Dark Emu" ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we look into the issue of how Australia's first people's exploited and shaped the landscape of this continent, including discussing whether they ever developed farming as proposed by Bruce Pascoe in his controversial work "Dark Emu"

Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 4: Laboratory recovery and sample curation We explore what is recovered by flotation and recovery rates, what we do next in the lab to extract plant remains, how we approach microfossil recovery and how to curate samples for the future

Archaeobotany & the challenges of the Anthropocene ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: An understanding of past plant use and landscapes from archaeology is increasingly being used to revitalise and improve resilience of cultures, economies and landscapes today in the face of an unpredictable future. We conclude our course by exploring the potential and future of archaeobotany in the wider world
Preparing your ARCS2080 Report (Assessment 3) In this workshop we discuss your final assessment and undertake exercises to help complete research for your report. Bring your questions!

We look into the seeds, chaff, straw and other elements that are preserved and discuss how assemblage analysis has transformed our understanding of past farming systems Plant trade, exchange and ancient globalisation ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plants have been widely traded for millennia and that trade drove significant cultural, economic and social transformations in the deep past. In this lecture we look at how archaeology can reconstruct plant trade and some key studies of its history from the origins of agriculture and sedentism, to the origins of states, globalisation and European colonialism Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 3: Fruits and nuts This week we look at the diversity of fruit, nut and wild seed structures we find in archaeology and explore further how we can use measurements and simple statistics to understand their identity and use Fuels and landscape management ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: As the main source of fuels for heat, cooking and industry, woodland and forests have been a major focus for landscape modification and management, and we look at how fuel histories can be constructed and what they tell us in a story now extending into the Pleistocene Module B, Plant Macrofossils. Practical 4: Indeterminate plant remains (wood and USO's) Indeterminate plant remains are fragments of larger plant structures, such as woody stems, that lack clear morphology and can't be resolved into whole items, as is the case with seeds. This week introduces the largely anatomical approaches we use to identify them, including wood charcoals and underground storage organs (think potatoes). Using wood charcoals as an example we also explore how to analyse indeterminate remains to reconstruct plant economies and landscape change Plants in ancient architecture, crafts and industry ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Plant products, including wood, fibres, waxes and stems have been used to produce everything from buildings and artefacts to clothing and ornaments. This week we survey the history of plants beyond food and intoxicants from the Pleistocene to modern world Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 1: Gathering for a living ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we start our exploration into the diverse ways humans have sustained themselves. We begin with foraging, also known as hunting and gathering, in which plants are harvested from natural vegetation growth. Sustaining humans since we first evolved, foraging has a deep and diverse history and with ever better methods we have increasingly detailed data from archaeological sites. Food is, however, not just about nutrition, and we also explore how food acquisition and sharing has developed as a key element of social behaviour Module C, Plant microfossils. Practical 2: Agriculture, cultivation and domestication ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture is the production of food and other resources from plants (and animals) that are managed and domesticated - meaning they are under human control and selection - and the world's vast population and complex global society is built on it. This week we consider the diversity of agricultural systems and the key concepts cultivation and domestication Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 1: Manual/bucket flotation At UQ's ATARC, we will explore how to extract plant remains via flotation. We begin by considering the aims of such recovery, its logistics and design, learning how to float samples using bucket flotation! This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear Global perspectives on the origins of agriculture ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Today's global society is built on the ancient and diverse foundations of ancient farming systems. This week we explore how archaeobotany has contributed to explaining where, when and why farming started at diverse places around the world in the deep past Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 2: Machine and hand pump flotation At UQ's ATARC we explore the wonders of machine and hand pump flotation, which allow larger scale processing of soil samples. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear Agriculture's ancient diversification and intensification ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: Agriculture has undergone significant changes as it spread and developed around the world. This week we look at some important moments of transformation, including the development of mixed farming, intensification, the secondary products revolution and the effects of trade on farming systems in some of the world's earliest examples of globalisation Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 3: Machine and hand pump flotation (continued) We continue and conclude our exploration of machine and hand pump flotation. This is an active practice in which you will get wet and muddy. See Blackboard for note on appropriate clothing and footwear Was Australia really a farming bystander? Evaluating Bruce Pascoe's "Dark Emu" ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: This week we look into the issue of how Australia's first people's exploited and shaped the landscape of this continent, including discussing whether they ever developed farming as proposed by Bruce Pascoe in his controversial work "Dark Emu" Module D, Recovering plant remains. Practical 4: Laboratory recovery and sample curation We explore what is recovered by flotation and recovery rates, what we do next in the lab to extract plant remains, how we approach microfossil recovery and how to curate samples for the future Archaeobotany & the challenges of the Anthropocene ONLINE CLASS IN UQ EXTEND: An understanding of past plant use and landscapes from archaeology is increasingly being used to revitalise and improve resilience of cultures, economies and landscapes today in the face of an unpredictable future. We conclude our course by exploring the potential and future of archaeobotany in the wider world Preparing your ARCS2080 Report (Assessment 3) In this workshop we discuss your final assessment and undertake exercises to help complete research for your report. Bring your questions! | Understand the general principles, methods and theory of archaeobotany Apply the analytical and theoretical approaches commonly used in archaeobotany to address research questions and understand human-plant interaction in the past Demonstrate critical understanding of the major methodological issues in archaeobotany and their impact on interpretation Understand the broader significance of archaeobotanical data to major archaeological and transdisciplinary questions and their appropriate application in these contexts

title: Biochemistry & Molecular Biology
prerequisite_raw: [BIOL1020 or BIOM1050 or BIOM1051 or CHEE1001 or BIOE1001] AND [CHEM1100 or CHEM1221 or CHEM1222]
description: This course will enable you to gain an understanding of the key concepts of biochemistry and molecular biology. At the end of the course you will have an understanding of how proteins can catalyse the chemical reactions that allow cells to function. To get to this point you will gain an understanding of: the nature of chemical reactions in biological systems; how genetic information is used to direct protein synthesis; how the structure of proteins is determined; how structure determines function; and how we understand the properties of enzymes. You will be able to reinforce and apply these concepts in laboratory practicals where you will purify and characterise a protein. An understanding of cellular processes at the molecular level is central to contemporary biological and biomedical sciences. The principles and practise of biochemistry and molecular biology form the basis for that understanding. The focus of BIOC2000 is the molecular components of cells: protein structure and function, enzymes and catalysis, DNA structure and function, and the links between disease and cellular metabolism. In conjunction with BIOC2001, BIOL2202 and MICR2000, BIOC2000 is a commonly recommended course in various Fields of Study in The University of Queensland Bachelor of Science program. BIOC2000 is essential for Biochemistry and Molecular Biology Major students.
learning_outcomes: Describe the chemical and physical forces driving biomolecular folding and interactions, then apply this knowledge to analysis of structures and functions of biomolecules.
Apply your understanding of transcription and translation to develop a molecular cloning strategy.
Interpret and analyse enzymatic function and kinetics.
Predict the impact of altering the components of a metabolic pathway.
Demonstrate theoretical and practical mastery of a range of crucial protein and DNA-related laboratory skills by working in the laboratory and producing a written report about your laboratory work.
Discuss, in an informed manner, the most revolutionary techniques in molecular biology and biochemistry such as restriction enzymes, PCR, GFP, and fusion proteins.
Relate observed biological phenomena to key underlying concepts of biochemistry.

Proteins and how we engineer them Lecturer: Justin Ridge
Protein Structure & Function Lecturer: Ben Schulz
Enzymology Lecturer: Elizabeth Gillam
Metabolism Lecturer: Justin Ridge

title: Advanced Biochemistry & Molecular Biology
description: This course will enable you to gain an understanding of the key concepts of biochemistry and molecular biology. At the end of the course you will have an understanding of how proteins can catalyse the chemical reactions that allow cells to function. To get to this point you will gain an understanding of: the nature of chemical reactions in biological systems; how genetic information is used to direct protein synthesis; how the structure of proteins is determined; how structure determines function; and how we understand the properties of enzymes. You will be able to reinforce and apply these concepts in extended laboratory practicals where you will complete a small project involving the expression, purification, quantification and characterisation of a protein. An understanding of cellular processes at the molecular level is central to contemporary biological and biomedical sciences. The principles and practise of biochemistry and molecular biology form the basis for that understanding. The focus of BIOC2900 is the molecular components of cells: protein structure and function, enzymes and catalysis, DNA structure and function, and cellular metabolism. BIOC2900, BIOL2900, BIOL2902 and MICR2900 form a suite of offerings in the Bachelor of Advanced Science program that contribute to the Biochemistry & Molecular Biology, Bioinformatics, Cell Biology, Genetics, and Microbiology specialisations.

Apply your understanding of transcription and translation to develop a molecular cloning strategy.
Interpret and analyse enzymatic function and kinetics.
Predict the impact of altering the components of a metabolic pathway.
Develop a workable experimental approach to achieve a specific goal.
Demonstrate theoretical and practical mastery of a range of laboratory skills.
Determine the most appropriate way to analyse and present data.
Discuss, in an informed manner, the most revolutionary techniques in molecular biology and biochemistry such as restriction enzymes, PCR, fusion proteins, and affinity chromatography.
Relate observed biological phenomena to key underlying concepts of biochemistry.

title: Structural & Synthetic Biology

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution

description: The understanding of molecular structure and function is of central importance to students undertaking a major in the biological or chemical fields. This course focuses on concepts of DNA/RNA, protein, lipid and carbohydrate relating structure to function. Some content and assignments are based on current literature describing recent DNA and protein structures and how structure can inform about function. Laboratory work will emphasize the techniques required to analyse biomolecules in the context of a student-driven synthetic biology project. In BIOC3000, you will further develop your understanding of biochemistry and molecular biology initiated in BIOC2000. You will learn about fundamental concepts of macromolecular structure and function, biochemical energy transduction, information transfer, systems integration and molecular evolution in the context of several “big questions” at the frontiers of research today. Parallel themes in each module will cover the structure and function of major biological molecules and fundamental biochemical functions common to all kingdoms of life. Examples relevant to human medicine will be included but the focus is broader than on simply human or mammalian biochemistry. Practicals focussed on synthetic and structural biology will integrate and synthesise your prior learning and introduce you to current research techniques.
learning_outcomes: Design and interpret simple experiments to examine the activity of enzymes.

Competently discuss the structure and function of important biological molecules including (but not exclusively) glycoproteins, enzymes, RNA and DNA binding proteins, ion channels and membrane trafficking proteins.

Suggest appropriate experimental tools that can be used to learn about the structure, function and activity of biological macromolecules, their capabilities and limitations. These include X-ray crystallography, cryo_EM, mass spectrometry, UV-vis spectrometry, spectrofluorimetry, and CRISPR gene editing.
Explain how synthetic biology approaches can be used to engineer novel enzymes and devices to address industrial challenges.
Competently discuss some of the big questions at the forefront of research in biochemistry and molecular biology today.
Demonstrate what constitutes good scientific practice.
Identify and use appropriate styles of communication for reporting scientific results for different purposes

learning_activities.topic: Structural Biology Practical Students will have the opportunity to learn and implement aspects of two commonly used research techniques for determining molecular structure – X-ray crystallography and electron cryo-microscopy (cryo-EM). The practical is focused on the structure of a particular protein – ferritin – which is an important protein for storage and transport of intracellular iron. In order to determine the structure of a protein by X-ray crystallography, it is first necessary to grow crystals of the protein of interest, and students will set up crystallisation trials for ferritin as well as a series of control proteins. Students will then learn about the process of obtaining diffraction data from crystals, and also about how to collect data by cryo-EM, which is particularly useful in cases where crystallisation fails. Students will then have the opportunity to process images of ferritin obtained by cryo-EM through to the point of obtaining a high resolution structure and will explore and interpret this structure further using molecular visualisation and model building

title: Human Molecular Genetics in Health & Disease
description: BIOC3003 is a research-informed course on the molecular genetics of human disease. The diverse topics include: single gene disorders, multifactorial disorders; cancer genetics; developmental genetics and birth defects; epigenetics; mobile genetic elements; and the identification and analysis of human disease genes. The course content will provide students with a contemporary overview of human genetic disorders, genetic variation and on how mutations are identified and assessed for their contribution to phenotype. In addition, you will explore key aspects of the clinical management of genetic diseases, from the genetic tools used in diagnosis, prognosis and treatment to the ethical considerations surrounding genetic testing. In the age of personalised genomic medicine and high-throughput “omics” approaches, obtaining a solid foundation in genetic principles at the molecular level has never been more important for understanding and, ultimately preventing, human disease. Hospital admissions, apart from childbirth, infections, and trauma injuries, are often the result of genetic disease. In addition, an individual's predisposition to diseases and their response to infection and wound healing have a strong genetic component. The diagnosis and treatment of genetic diseases require a thorough understanding of molecular genetics and molecular biology. This course consists of seven modules that will cover the most significant areas of modern molecular genetics.
learning_outcomes: Explain the ethical issues that pertain to genetic testing and to the use of genetic data, including genomics.
Describe and discuss the contribution of genetic differences to predisposition to disease.
Justify that the one gene = one disease model is an oversimplification.
Describe the biochemical and molecular biology processes underpinning genetic disorders.
Apply some the experimental approaches that are used to determine the genetic basis of human phenotypes, including diseases, and to be able to articulate how these methods are employed.
Explain how multiple loci/alleles/genes contribute to multi-factorial diseases or predisposition to disease.
Explain how genetics underpins the development of cancers and developmental disorders.
Explain how epigenetics contributes to human health and disease.
Critically evaluate the scientific literature pertaining to human genetics in health and disease.
topics: There are 3 hours of lecture content per week: a 1 hour lecture on Monday and a 2 hour lecture on Friday. Generally each module will begin with a 2 hour Friday lecture, and include a 1 hour Monday lecture and 2 hour Wednesday lecture the following week. In between modules there are dedicated 1 hour Monday lectures to introduce workshops and provide feedback on assessment items and exam preparation

Each workshop is run twice (Monday and Thursday) - check your timetable to see which session you have been allocated. Regular formative assessment, including automatically marked quizzes, will be used during the workshops to provide feedback on progress. Workshops are also supported by dedicated lectures in the usual Monday lecture slot. Refer to the detailed timetable on blackboard for further details

learning_activities.topic: Structural Biology Practical Students will have the opportunity to learn and implement aspects of two commonly used research techniques for determining molecular structure – X-ray crystallography and electron cryo-microscopy (cryo-EM). The practical is focused on the structure of a particular protein – ferritin – which is an important protein for storage and transport of intracellular iron. In order to determine the structure of a protein by X-ray crystallography, it is first necessary to grow crystals of the protein of interest, and students will set up crystallisation trials for ferritin as well as a series of control proteins. Students will then learn about the process of obtaining diffraction data from crystals, and also about how to collect data by cryo-EM, which is particularly useful in cases where crystallisation fails. Students will then have the opportunity to process images of ferritin obtained by cryo-EM through to the point of obtaining a high resolution structure and will explore and interpret this structure further using molecular visualisation and model building

title: Introduction to Molecular Biology Laboratory

assessments.category: Creative Production/ Exhibition, Paper/ Report/ Annotation

description: This course will introduce you to key skills required to carry out modern molecular biological research. By the end of this course, you will have an understanding of how many commonly used molecular biological techniques work, as well as their advantages and limitations, together with the fundamentals of experimental design and the ethical considerations of research. To get to this point you will reinforce and apply material learnt in lectures and tutorials in regular laboratory practical sessions where the detection, quantification, characterisation, and purification of proteins and nucleic acids will be explored. BIOC6001 Introduction to Molecular Biology Laboratory has been designed to meet the needs of people from a variety of professional backgrounds, but in particular those undertaking training in the biological sciences including molecular biology, biotechnology and bioinformatics. The main emphasis of this course is to provide a deeper understanding of the most commonly used techniques in molecular biology and will also provide hands-on experience of these laboratory techniques. ﾠCompletion of this course will provide the foundational knowledge required for BIOC7001.
learning_outcomes: Understand and articulate the theoretical basis of common molecular biological techniques.
Competently carry out common techniques in the laboratory.
Describe and apply the principles of basic experimental design.
Apply and describe practices that demonstrate scientific ethics.
Collect, interpret and report molecular biological data.
Present findings in common forms of scientific communication.

title: Introduction to Proteins & Nucleic Acids
coordinator: Lectures and tutorials for Module 1: Weeks 1-3 of semester. A/Prof Jack Wang (lectures 1-7) will provide a series of lectures with interwoven tutorials.
description: This course will introduce you to the basics of molecular biology, covering both nucleic acid and proteins, to prepare you for the more advanced courses in the program. The emphasis will be on understanding the key roles of nucleic acids and proteins in biology, and how we characterise them. None, although we travel from the basics to more advanced material at a fairly brisk pace. Engineers, computer scientists, school teachers and nurses have completed similarﾠcourses and done quite well, as have medical doctors, physiotherapists, veterinarians and academics. We prefer students to have a solid biology background (at least senior high school biology), but you need not have done any molecular biology.
learning_outcomes: Interpret and articulate advanced concepts of nucleic acid structure and function (DNA and RNA) and protein biochemistry (protein structure and function).
Interpret and competently discuss current developments in genetics, epigenetics, genomics, genetic engineering, molecular biology and protein biochemistry.
Critically analyse and explain advancements in sequencing and detection methodologies for nucleic acids and proteins.
Evaluate how recombinant DNA technologies are used in genetic engineering.
Articulate the ethical issues associated with discoveries and their applications in molecular genetics.
Competently find information in the field of molecular genetics using electronic and other resources and be able to evaluate the veracity of this information.

title: Advanced Proteins & Nucleic Acids

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: BIOC6040 or equivalent prior knowledge

description: The primary aim of this course is to examine how biological processes are studied at a molecular level. The emphasis is on the experimental approaches that are used to study proteins and nucleic acids: their identification, structural characterisation, and functional analysis (including bioinformatics). Case studies of groundbreaking, modern experiments will be used to illustrate how different biological problems have been dissected at the molecular level.
learning_outcomes: Correctly explain the theory and practice of modern protein analysis techniques to scientist peers using appropriate visual and oral modes of presentation.
Search protein and literature databases to obtain information about proteins and the experiments used to discover and analyse them.
Read, interpret, and re-present primary literature to peers.
Employ critical analysis of the methods, results, and discussion in primary literature (specifically in studies that address protein structure and function).
Use nucleic acid and protein bioinformatics tools to analyse problems in protein biology, biochemistry, and structure; and describe the use of these tools to an audience.
Be able to propose and justify a workable experimental design for the analysis of a protein structure and/or function.
Provide constructive criticism to a peer on their written and oral presentations and use feedback to make reflective value judgements about the quality of your own written and oral work.

title: Advanced Molecular Biology Laboratory
description: This course allows students to gain core molecular biological laboratory skills. This course focuses on techniques and protocols used in molecular biology providing an in-depth understanding of the principles behind them. Students will also gain experience in the interpretation of protocols, dealing with and interconverting units, and communicating results in a number of commonly used means. This course is designed for students who will soon be, or are currently, undertaking a lab-based research project and is one of the core courses of the Master of Molecular Biology programme. This course builds on the concepts and experience gained in BIOC6001. The course will cover advanced aspects of working with both nucleic acids and proteins in the laboratory.
learning_outcomes: Maintain accurate and legible records of experimental procedures.
Interpret and critically appraise experimental results.
Develop workable plans to achieve experimental goals utilising molecular biology.
topics: Weekly Lectures These lectures will introduce advanced concepts in molecular biology. You will learn how to specifically apply key molecular biological protocols. A more detailed timetable will be posted to Blackboard

description: The understanding of molecular structure and function is of central importance to students undertaking a major in the biological or chemical fields. This course focuses on concepts of DNA/RNA, protein, lipid and carbohydrate relating structure to function. Some content and assignments are based on current literature describing recent DNA and protein structures and how structure can inform about function. Laboratory work will emphasize the techniques required to analyse biomolecules in the context of an extended student-driven synthetic biology project. Students will explore modern methods in structural biology in a second extended laboratory project. In BIOC7100, you will further develop your understanding of biochemistry and molecular biology. You will learn about fundamental concepts of macromolecular structure and function, biochemical energy transduction, information transfer, systems integration and molecular evolution in the context of several “big questions” at the frontiers of research today. Parallel themes in each module will cover the structure and function of major biological molecules and fundamental biochemical functions common to all kingdoms of life. Examples relevant to human medicine will be included but the focus is broader than on simply human or mammalian biochemistry. Practicals focussed on synthetic and structural biology will integrate and synthesise your prior learning and introduce you to current research techniques.

title: Bioprocess Engineering
prerequisite_raw: (BIOE1001 or CHEE1001 or BIOL1020) and CHEE2001

assessments.task: Exam In-Semester Outside Scheduled Class Time

description: Biochemical and biomolecular engineering principles, biomolecular reactions, kinetics and industrial applications. Analysis of engineered biomolecular systems. Design principles of biomolecular production. Downstream bioprocessing including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration & purification, recombinant protein refolding and recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Scale-up considerations. This course applies chemical and bioprocess engineering principles to bioreactor design and operation. Two central themes are cell growth and biopharmaceutical production. The course covers aspects of Bioreaction engineering and design, processing including upstream and downstream, and analysis. The course first covers types of biomolecules and methods of design, before addressing the production and processing of molecules using cells, bioreactors, and downstream recovery units. Downstream processing, also known as recovery, is addressed in detail including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration and purification operations and design, recombinant protein recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Analytical methods for biomolecules are also covered to ensure product form and efficacy. A series of industry guest lectures will be made to show how the different course elements integrate to enable the engineer to design and optimise the entire process in bioprocess engineering.
learning_outcomes: Integrate knowledge of biochemistry, microbial growth, enzyme kinetics and biotechnology for analysing the performance of bioreactor processes.
Critically assess bioprocessing applications based on analytical data and how they can be optimised.
Apply bioprocessing design principles to fermentation, sterilisation and downstream processing equipment.
Operate and monitor bioreactor processes hands-on.
topics: Module 1 - Introduction Introduction to the course and the background principles of bioprocess engineering. Enzyme kinetics and inhibition
Module 2 - Stoichiometry and Kinetics Cell metabolism, stoichiometric mass balance concepts, Monod kinetics, cell maintenance, introduction to metabolic flux analysis
Module 3 - Bioreactor design Bioreactor design principles and concepts for different bioreactor configurations. Growth of bacteria vs mammalian cells, mass transfer limitations
Module 4 - Industrial applications Application of bioprocess engineering: industry guest lectures, biopharmaceuticals, up/down stream processing

search_blob: BIOE4020 Bioprocess Engineering | Biochemical and biomolecular engineering principles, biomolecular reactions, kinetics and industrial applications. Analysis of engineered biomolecular systems. Design principles of biomolecular production. Downstream bioprocessing including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration & purification, recombinant protein refolding and recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Scale-up considerations. This course applies chemical and bioprocess engineering principles to bioreactor design and operation. Two central themes are cell growth and biopharmaceutical production. The course covers aspects of Bioreaction engineering and design, processing including upstream and downstream, and analysis. The course first covers types of biomolecules and methods of design, before addressing the production and processing of molecules using cells, bioreactors, and downstream recovery units. Downstream processing, also known as recovery, is addressed in detail including solid-liquid separations, cell disruption, product concentration and purification operations and design, recombinant protein recovery, product stabilisation and formulation. Analytical methods for biomolecules are also covered to ensure product form and efficacy. A series of industry guest lectures will be made to show how the different course elements integrate to enable the engineer to design and optimise the entire process in bioprocess engineering. | and in-class exercises Tutorial problems and in-class exercises associated with each week's lecture content Module 1 - Introduction Introduction to the course and the background principles of bioprocess engineering. Enzyme kinetics and inhibition Module 2 - Stoichiometry and Kinetics Cell metabolism, stoichiometric mass balance concepts, Monod kinetics, cell maintenance, introduction to metabolic flux analysis Module 3 - Bioreactor design Bioreactor design principles and concepts for different bioreactor configurations. Growth of bacteria vs mammalian cells, mass transfer limitations Module 4 - Industrial applications Application of bioprocess engineering: industry guest lectures, biopharmaceuticals, up/down stream processing Prac activities Two pracs will be conducted, involving operating lab-scale bioreactors and monitoring them through assessing the biomass growth rate, as well as the oxygen transfer and uptake rate and other relevant process parameters Field Trip A guided field trip will take place to Thermofisher/Patheon in 37 Kent St, Woolloongabba QLD 4102, with an accompanying report. The class will split into 2 groups and the tour will be 2h | Integrate knowledge of biochemistry, microbial growth, enzyme kinetics and biotechnology for analysing the performance of bioreactor processes. Critically assess bioprocessing applications based on analytical data and how they can be optimised. Apply bioprocessing design principles to fermentation, sterilisation and downstream processing equipment. Operate and monitor bioreactor processes hands-on.

title: Principles of Biomedical & Bioprocess Engineering
prerequisite_raw: High School Chemistry or High School Biology or CHEM1090

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: High School Chemistry or High School Biology or CHEM1090

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Practical/ Demonstration

description: Introduction to biochemical and microbiological principles of relevance to engineers: cell biology, metabolism, molecular aspects of gene expression, structure and functions of biological molecules. Applications to industrial processes. Many chemical engineering processes and products depend on biological processes. Increasingly these processes and products are developed and produced using modern advanced biotechnology techniques. Biomedical engineering also relies on cell and molecular biology, associated with a deep understanding of systems physiology. In this course you will receive an introduction to biological principles of relevance to engineers, including cell biology, metabolism, molecular aspects of gene expression, structure and functions of biological molecules including proteins and DNA, systems physiology and cell signaling. You will learn how scientific principles underpin advances in biomedical and bioprocess engineering. Examples of the application of these principles to industrial processes will be presented. This course provides you with the basic knowledge required for multidisciplinary projects in bioprocess and biomedical engineering, and will provide the background to proceed further in courses associated with bioengineering.
learning_outcomes: Explain and apply concepts of microbiology, biochemistry and cell and molecular biology necessary for further studies in biomedical or bioprocess engineering: Describe the biochemical structures of biomolecules relevant to their use in biotechnological processes; Explain the basic growth characteristics and metabolism of engineered organisms; Describe the use of molecular biological techniques in biotechnology; Detail the principles of systems physiology and cell signaling underpinning biomedical engineering applications; Describe the role of biotechnology and bioengineering fundamentals in a wider industrial and societal context.

Describe the techniques used for observing, monitoring and engineering cultures, cells and cell metabolism. Use practical biological engineering laboratory skills: Demonstrate good, safe experimental techniques in biological engineering practical classes; Observe, record and interpret experimental data; Integrate theoretical knowledge and experimental results into a broader understanding of the course material.

Recognise the diversity of biomedical and bioprocess engineering products and processes and identify the science that underpins them; integrate knowledge gained from different methods of learning to gain an overall understanding of bioprocess and biomedical engineering applications.
topics: Lectures covering scientific fundamentals of biology relevant to engineering, and their application in biomedical and bioprocess engineering

title: Fundamentals of Neuroengineering

assessments.category: Computer Code, Practical/ Demonstration, Tutorial/ Problem Set
Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Project

description: The electrical activity within the nervous system represents information about the external world, the internal state, and the commands to control the body. This transformation between the information represented in the neural pathways and the electrical activity of neurons is referred to as the neural code. This course provides an overview of the underlying biological processes that give rise to neural activity, the models of neural encoding and decoding including leading hypotheses about information representation, and the extension of these models to population encoding in neural circuits. This course is organized into thematic sections with formal lectures on critical topics to gain exposure to state-of-the-art neural engineering. This is an interdisciplinary course that links subject matter across multiple fields. Students should attend sessions in person to maximally engage with the content.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate how neurons generate and propagate electrical signals and the basic properties of signal transmission in neural circuits
Critique and select relevant frameworks for analysing neural data
Implement signal processing algorithms for practical problems involving neuroscience
Demonstrate the need of engineering frameworks for large scale neural modelling

Critically analyse information independently acquired from diverse sources (e.g., research articles, books, patents, company product data sheets) in providing solutions to real-world neuroscience problems.
Effectively communicate (using oral and written communication and appropriate figures and visualizations) the real-world implementation of analytical frameworks in neuroscience
Orally present and report a research project on neural signal processing
topics: Technical content delivery

title: Cell and Tissue Engineering

assessments.task: C&T Engineering Innovation Project Proposal and Project Pitch Team or group-based

description: Introduction to mammalian cell & tissue engineering for human therapeutic applications. Design and production of functional tissue units, therapeutic cells and biomolecules. Clinical applications and regulatory issues. Cell and Tissue Engineering (C&TE) is a constantly evolving field with the potential to deliver therapeutic solutions that lie beyond the limitations of traditional pharmaceutical treatments and biomaterials.
learning_outcomes: Knowledge of the field: Demonstrate a general understanding of the breadth and scope of the field of cell tissue and engineering, as well as current trends in the field.
Independent Learning: Manage personal independent research and learning through pro-active and responsible use of resources such as the library and internet.
Ethical and Social Impact: Comprehend the applicable ethical and social impacts of the field of cell and tissue engineering.
Scientific Communication: Conduct effective scientific written and oral communication of research findings. Employ correct referencing and scientific structure in reports and presentations.
Group work: Improve laboratory-based teamwork skills through practice, reflection, and feedback.

title: Medical Device Engineering

assessments.task: 1. Structured Peer Feedback on Team Presentations

description: This course is aimed at exposing engineering students to the innovation and development processes around medical devices. It aims at covering aspects in system engineering and design principles, medical device regulation, quality manufacturing systems, biomedical engineering ethics, and clinical validation. Students will also gain an appreciation on general working principles (engineering subsystems and associated biophysical processes) of selected medical devices across different risk categorization levels (Class-I-III), and current research trends. This course will provide detailed coverage of the engineering aspects of medical devices. Key topics include:
learning_outcomes: Analyse and evaluate medical devices by identifying their medical need, value proposition, underlying medical condition, existing and emerging solutions, and operating principles, while assessing the technical basis of their functionality and subsystem integration.

Critically investigate and evaluate medical devices by appraising their intended purpose, efficacy & efficiency in treating or diagnosing medical conditions, technical sub-components, safety risks and limitations, ethical considerations, and research directions.

Apply specialized engineering knowledge and practices to ideate, design, prototype, and develop a medical device, including the identification of user needs, translation into technical requirements, validation and verification, application of relevant standards, and effective documentation practices to ensure compliance with Quality Management Systems.

Explain in detail, the innovation and product development cycle of medical devices, including considerations for compliance with regulatory agencies and international standards across different classifications.

Communicate effectively, both orally and in writing, the purpose, operation, technical functionality, regulatory considerations, and research directions of selected medical devices to technical, clinical, and lay audiences.
Demonstrate the ability to implement professional engineering principles while effectively collaborating in a multi-disciplinary team, showing respect for diverse ideas, work styles, and backgrounds.
Appraise and provide constructive feedback to peers’ work by employing a SPARK (Specific, Prescriptive, Actionable, Referenced and Kind) approach.
topics: Active learning Students will be pointed to recommended articles, book chapters, and material that are relevant to the course's learning objectives. It is expected that students spend a good amount of time studying the recommended material. Students will be expected to do short presentations during scheduled class, and participate in discussion forums based on the recommended material

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: There are 4 hours of scheduled activities in this course. Irrespective of how they are labelled in the timetable, these sessions will include a mix of lecture, studio and discussion-style activities. These sessions will include presentations of the course material and discussions, with active student participation encouraged. Background material will be provided by the lecturer. Invited lecturers/seminars are also planned

Active learning Students will be pointed to recommended articles, book chapters, and material that are relevant to the course's learning objectives. It is expected that students spend a good amount of time studying the recommended material. Students will be expected to do short presentations during scheduled class, and participate in discussion forums based on the recommended material

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 5 (09 Mar - 29 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Invited talks Medical devices engineering is a very comprehensive and trans-disciplinary field. To give students the opportunity to learn from different stakeholders (e.g. clinicians, patients, industry representatives), invited lectures / seminars will be organized throughout the semester. These will happen during scheduled class hours, unless otherwise announced

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 12 (09 Mar - 24 May)
learning_activities.topic: Team project Students will work on the conceptual design of a medical device based on a real clinical need. Students will apply medical device engineering practices learned throughout the course. Students will work in teams of 3-4 members. A major assessment component of this course is related to this activity

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 8 (09 Mar - 26 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Field excursions One or two field excursions, within short reach of UQ (e.g. Metro North Health facilities), are planned within scheduled hours. Once confirmed, a detailed schedule of activities will be available in Blackboard

title: Introductory Biology

assessments.task: Inquiry and Synthesis in the AI Era - Part 1: Written
Inquiry and Synthesis in the AI Era – Part 2: In-Class assessment

description: In this course, students will examine how living things, from the building blocks of cells and the molecules that underpin them, to diverse organisms and ecosystems, persist and thrive by continuously responding to change. Through an integrated journey spanning biochemistry, cell structure, genetics, energy transformations, and evolutionary history, the course reveals how biological systems sense, adapt, and evolve. The narrative weaves together fundamental cellular mechanisms and the origins of biodiversity. By recognizing evolutionary innovations and physiological strategies, students will understand life’s remarkable flexibility in the face of shifting environments. This multidisciplinary approach highlights both the unity and diversity of life, underscoring the importance of adaptation as a central theme in biology. Students may complete BIOL1000 and BIOL1020 concurrently, or BIOL1000 prior to completing BIOL1020, which is recommended.
learning_outcomes: Explain the structure and function of cells, including organelles and macromolecules, as foundations of life.
Describe energy flow in biological systems, including photosynthesis and cellular respiration.
Summarise principles of genetics, inheritance, and reproduction in living organisms.
Outline the diversity of life and evolutionary principles underpinning biological classification.
Describe core ecological concepts, including organism-environment interactions, energy transfer, and ecosystem dynamics.
Critically analyse and interpret biological data using digital and visual tools, including the ethical use of Artificial Intelligence (AI), to evaluate scientific evidence.

Recognise how biological knowledge, including Indigenous knowledges and perspectives of Country, informs debates on conservation, environment, and human impacts, fostering inclusive and culturally aware understanding.
topics: (Module 1) Each week you will attend a 2 hour interactive lecture. Module 1 = Responding to change in the cellular environment
(Module 2) This lecture is the start of Module 2 - Responding to Change in the Natural Environment. This module will be lead by Dr. Nicola Browne
(Module 3) This week is the start of Module 3 = Responding to change in the evolutionary environment. Module 3 is led by Ass. Prof Anthony Young
Revision lecture Revision lecture

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 4
learning_activities.topic: (Module 1) Each week you will attend a 2 hour interactive lecture. Module 1 = Responding to change in the cellular environment

Workshops (Module 1) Each week you will also attend a 2 hour workshop where you will be guided through activities related to the corresponding week's lecture. These workshops have been designed to help you understand key concepts. In week 1, you will be given a workbook, which you are expected to fill out during your workshop time. These workbooks are not graded, but will be an excellent revision guide for the final exam (the course hurdle). Hence, the more work and detail you put into your workbook, the more detailed revision guide you will have! In week 1 we will also discuss how to start work on your AI assessment. Weeks 2,3,4 will start with your MCQ quiz

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 5 To Week 8
learning_activities.topic: (Module 2) This lecture is the start of Module 2 - Responding to Change in the Natural Environment. This module will be lead by Dr. Nicola Browne

Workshops (Module 2) Over these four weeks, you will complete three MCQs at the start of workshops 6, 7 and 8. In week 5, you will also be provided time to work on your AI assessment and seek feedback on your progress

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 9 To Week 12
learning_activities.topic: (Module 3) This week is the start of Module 3 = Responding to change in the evolutionary environment. Module 3 is led by Ass. Prof Anthony Young

Workshops (Module 3) Over these four weeks, you will complete three MCQs at the start of workshops 10, 11 and 12. In week 9, you will also be provided time to work on your AI assessment and seek feedback on your progress
Revision lecture Revision lecture

Final workshop In this final workshop you will complete part of your AI assessment. There will also be time for reflection on your workbooks and feedback on how to improve your workbooks to enhance this revision tool for your end of semester exam
