There are four Smart Skills Workshops across the semester: Week 2: Biomedical Practices Smart Skills Workshop Week 5: Nervous System Smart Skills Workshop Week 9: Cardiovascular System Smart Skills Workshop Week 12: Respiratory System Smart Skills Workshop Module 2 - Nervous System [Coakley] Module 2 will be delivered by Dr Sean Coakley via 6 Lectures, one Team Skills Workshop, and one Smart Skills Workshop. In this module, you will be introduced to the structure and function of the nervous system. This incredibly complex network of billions of neurons controls every aspect of the human condition. You will learn about the cells that make up the nervous system, how their structure is related to their function and how they send electrical signals throughout the body to control every conscious and unconscious action we take Biomed Cohort Connections Week Scheduled during the usual BIOM001 Lecture and Workshop times, this week of co-curricular activities will include fun and informative opportunities for you to engage with students in your cohort and across the Biomedical Science program, student societies, alumni, course teaching staff, and the wider biomed community at SBMS Module 3 - Cardiovascular System [Thomas] Module 3 will be delivered by Professor Wally Thomas via 6 Lectures, one Team Skills Workshop, and one Smart Skills Workshop. In this module you will explore the physiology and anatomy of the cardiovascular system and the remarkable pump, the heart, that propels blood through our vessels. You will be introduced to how cardiac muscle contracts, how blood pressure is controlled and explore the huge burden of cardiovascular disease on our healthcare system Module 4 - Respiratory System [Faber] Module 4 will be delivered by Dr Luli Faber via 6 Lectures, one Team Skills Workshop, and one Smart Skills Workshop. In this module you will explore why we breathe, what happens if we don't and the important jobs of our incredible respiratory system | Explain how scientific knowledge and progress is developed in biomedical science. Identify and explain how the structure and function of the nervous system coordinate the body's response to internal and external stimuli. Identify and explain how the anatomical structures of the heart and blood vessels relate to their physiological functions. Identify and explain the relationships between the anatomical features of the respiratory system and its physiological functions. Work and learn collaboratively in group settings, valuing and responding respectfully to diverse abilities, backgrounds, and perspectives. Evaluate and reflect on your learning experiences throughout the course to identify strengths and areas for development in your understanding, learning strategies, and collaborative practice.

title: Advanced Molecular Cell Biology I
description: This course is intended to be a comprehensive introduction to Cell Biology. We will discuss the vast diversity of cells and how their structures subserve their functions. This course has an enrolment capacity of 40 students. Please contact enquiries@scmb.uq.edu.au for all enrolment queries . This course is intended to be a comprehensive introduction to cell biology. Some of the main concepts covered include:
learning_outcomes: Describe the major components of cells, their molecular structures, and functions, and understand intracellular compartmentalization
Explain how cells respond to external signals using specific examples
Competently complete experimental techniques in cell biology including the cell culture, microscopy, handling cells, and DNA analysis.
Clearly communicate experimental results through the accurate recording and evaluation of experimental observations

title: Advanced Ecology
description: Ecology of individual organisms, populations, communities and ecosystems. Students will gain foundational knowledge and quantitative skills in ecology through local and global case studies and critical analyses of conceptual controversies in ecology, to illustrate ecological debate and the way in which ecological knowledge is advanced. Students will also participate in advanced topics discussion to see how ecology is applied in research. Watch this video for an introduction to studying BIOL2910. Note that BIOL2910 is taught concurrently with BIOL2010. You should not enrol in both courses.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate understanding of key ecological principles, processes, and patterns
Describe and evaluate different experimental approaches used in various natural systems
Apply algorithms on ecological data to draw conclusions about trends, patterns, or relationships in ecology
Read and appraise ecological literature critically
Propose and execute experiments to test original hypotheses relevant to ecology
Develop a meaningful external stakeholder engagement strategy aimed at improving community knowledge about an aspect in ecology

title: Introductory Cellular Physiology

assessments.task: Invigilated On Campus End of Semester Examination

description: The course covers the fundamental concepts that allow complex organisms to function, with some focus on the human body & other higher organisms. Key concepts include basic cellular transport & signalling mechanisms, principles of biochemistry, neuronal structure & function, motor mechanisms & locomotion, circulation & gas exchange, and the endocrine system. Of key importance is the integration of concepts as they apply to the structure and function of different regions of the entire organism. BIOM1051 ('Introductory Cellular Physiology') is an integratedﾠbiology course offered by the School of Biomedical Sciences and theﾠSchool of Chemistry & Molecular Biosciences. It presents a comprehensive overview of how structure and function are integrated at all levels from the cell to the organism.
learning_outcomes: Explain and discuss specific examples of biological research in different communication modes and forums.
Demonstrate core competency in basic biological laboratory techniques.
Demonstrate sound scientific reasoning in the context of practical sessions.
Demonstrate an appreciation of the processes that scientists use to discover and verify new knowledge.
Demonstrate quantitative skills (QS) and their application in biology.
Work effectively in teams to achieve sound academic outcomes.
Demonstrate an understanding of the control, integration and coordination mechanisms that are required to maintain a functional organism.
Demonstrate an appreciation that diversity is reflected through biological adaptation among species.
Describe the structure and function of major organ systems.
Demonstrate an understanding of how pathways of intracellular and extracellular communication are integral to homeostasis.
Demonstrate an appreciation of the biochemical basis of biological function.
Describe the physical and electrochemical properties of cell membranes in terms of excitable and non-excitable cells.
Demonstrate how support and movement are interrelated.
Relate the internal transport and delivery of gases, nutrients and messengers to the organ systems of relevant species.
Demonstrate an appreciation of the interdisciplinary nature of biological systems.

title: Biology for Human Movement & Nutrition Sciences

assessments.task: Invigilated On Campus End of Semester Examination

description: The course covers the fundamental concepts that allow the human body to function, with an approach that is appropriate for students who did not study Senior Chemistry at school. Key areas of study include fundamental biochemistry & molecular biology, basic cell function, neuronal structure & function, motor mechanisms, locomotion & development, circulation & gas exchange, and the endocrine system. Homeostasis and the integration of concepts as they apply to the structure and function of different regions of the entire human body are of key importance. BIOM1050 (Biology for Human Movement & Nutrition Sciences) is a first year biology course specifically designed for students who have not completed senior chemistry at high school. Fundamental chemical concepts will be integrated with the consideration of biological concepts in relevant places throughout the course. The course isﾠoffered by the School of Biomedical Sciences (SBMS) with additional input from School of Chemistry and Molecular Biosciences (SCMB).
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate an understanding of how pathways of intracellular and extracellular communication are integral to homeostasis
Demonstrate an understanding of the essential chemical basis governing biological and molecular processes in the human body
Demonstrate an understanding of the control, integration and coordination mechanisms that are required to maintain a functional human body
Describe the structure and function of major organ systems
Demonstrate how support and movement are interrelated during growth and development
Relate the internal transport and delivery of gases, nutrients and messengers to the organ systems
Demonstrate core competency in basic biological laboratory techniques
Demonstrate sound scientific reasoning in the context of practical sessions
Demonstrate quantitative skills and their application in biology

description: BIOM1070 provides students with a foundation in the understanding of cell biology and biochemistry, organisation of the human body, support and movement, and regulation and integration of the body. BIOM1070 (Biology for Health Sciences Part A) is a first-year biology course designed to provide students with a fundamental understanding of:
learning_outcomes: Explain how pathways of intracellular and extracellular communication are integral to homeostasis
Explain the essential chemical basis governing biological and molecular processes in the human body
Explain the control, integration and coordination mechanisms that are required to maintain a functional human body
Define the principal functions of some human organ systems and summarise the relationships between the various organ systems
Communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology
Identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of some of the human organ systems
Work scientifically, propose explanations, test hypotheses and draw conclusions about human anatomy and physiology through practical experimentation
Demonstrate an awareness of, and respect for, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people’s values, cultures, and knowledges and how this relates to being a contemporary health practitioner
topics: Module 1: Cell Biology In this module (12 lectures), students will gain a comprehensive understanding of cell biology. Topics include the structure and function of eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells, cell membrane dynamics, and the principles of osmosis. The course also delves into molecular biology, focusing on nucleic acids, proteins, and cellular energy processes. These lectures provide a theoretical understanding required for practical classes throughout the semester

Module 2: Organisation of the Human Body In Module 2 (2 lectures), students will learn about the hierarchical structure in biology, from cells to organ systems. This module introduces essential terminology and gives a foundational overview of gross anatomy, crucial for understanding the complexity of living organisms

Module 3: Nervous System In Module 3 (9 lectures), students will delve into the anatomy and physiology of the Central and Peripheral Nervous System. This module covers a range of topics, including the intricacies of reflexes, the structure and function of nervous system cells, and the fundamentals of resting membrane potential. Students will gain a deep understanding of action potentials, synaptic transmission, cell communication, and receptors. The module also explores the Autonomic Nervous System

Module 4: Musculoskeletal System Module 4 (6 lectures) provides an in-depth introduction to the Musculoskeletal System. This module begins with an overview of the system's structure and function. It delves into the types of bones and bone physiology, providing a comprehensive understanding of the skeletal framework. The module further explores the neuromuscular junction, muscle contraction, and the interaction between muscles and bones. Students will also learn about muscle agonists, antagonists, synergists, and stabilisers alongside joint anatomy and biomechanics

Module 5: Endocrine System Module 5 (6 lectures) introduces students to the Endocrine System. This module begins with a general overview of the system, focusing on its crucial role in bodily functions. Students will explore key endocrine glands, including the pituitary, pineal, hypothalamus, thyroid, and parathyroid glands. The module also covers the adrenal glands, delving into their structure and function. A significant focus is placed on reproductive endocrinology, addressing the hormonal aspects of reproduction. Additionally, the module examines the endocrine regulation of metabolism and energy balance, offering insights into how hormones influence these vital processes

These lectures provide a theoretical understanding required for practical classes throughout the semester

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Nursing and Midwifery
description: BIOM1060 provides nursing and midwifery students with an opportunity to develop an understanding of the interdependence of structure & function in humans at the cellular, tissues, organ, organ system and organism levels of function. BIOM1060 is the first in series of biomedical science courses for Nursing and Midwifery students. In this course you will learn the foundations of biomedical science, with a focus on anatomy and physiology. The course begins with an introduction to human structure and function, and the basic principles of cell biology. You will then investigate the anatomy and physiology of the musculoskeletal, nervous, cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, renal and reproductive systems. The knowledge and skills obtained in this course will allow you to understand aspects of normal human organ and body system structure and function, and aid in the subsequent recognition and understanding of abnormal function in disease and application of various treatment strategies for acute and chronic illnesses.
learning_outcomes: describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute some of the human organ systems

identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of some of the human organ systems on human cadaveric specimens and communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology
define the principal functions of human organ systems and summarise the relationships between the various organ systems

identify and explain the importance of homeostasis in human survival, including the importance of the cellular environment and the cell membrane, cellular communication, and integration of cellular responses

describe the link between human biology and disease processes and predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure, within an ethical framework of respect for both living and deceased humans
explain how anatomical and physiological changes occur throughout the life cycle, including pregnancy, embryology, and foetal development
work scientifically, propose explanations, test hypotheses and draw conclusions about human anatomy and physiology through practical experimentation
demonstrate the importance of physiological tests used in health care settings
reflect effectively on your learning in biomedical science.
topics: Module 1: Cells [Ainscough] In this module, you will learn about: The fundamentals of A&P Cell structure and function Membrane transport This content will be covered in a revision lecture in week 2

Module 5: Respiratory system [Suen] In this module, you will learn about: Respiratory anatomy; Gas exchange and transport; Respiratory mechanics and lung volumes; Control of respiration

title: Integrative Cell & Tissue Biology
prerequisite_raw: BIOL1040 or BIOL1050 or (BIOM1001 and BIOM1002) or (BIOM1070 and BIOM1071)
description: Students will develop an understanding of how cells associate & interact to fulfill their normal functions in tissues & organs of the human body. This will include the basic principles of integration & regulation that underpin normal tissue biology. BIOM2011 is integrative cell and tissue biology . The course will focus on the structure of cells and cell membranes and the functional importance of this. In particular, fundamental aspects of how cells respond to the environment, including neighbouring cells and the extracellular environment will be covered. This includes ion channels in the membranes and ionic movements across these membranes and the control of cell and tissue function in response to changes in channel states and different conditions in the cell's environment.
learning_outcomes: Explain the cellular and molecular processes that lead to coordinated and regulated activity in tissues of the body
Recognise and explain the basic role of the nervous system and endocrine systems as effectors in maintaining homeostasis
Describe the specialization of cell structures and how this relates to function
Explain how changes at the cellular level meet the demand of the host tissue
Demonstrate competency and creativity in experimental design and scientific reasoning
Participate in, and explain the experimental process and role of model systems in discovery of new knowledge

: Nernst Potential Determination of the relationship between [K+] and membrane potential and determination of unknown [K+]. In this practical, you will construct a standard curve to fit the relationship between known [K+] concentration on either side of an artificial membrane and the electrical potential set across it. You will be supplied with an "unknown" solution, and will have to determine its [K+] from the curve you constructed. You will also prepare a standard physiological solution and determine the accuracy of your solution based on the membrane potential you record. THIS PRACTICAL HAS ASSESSMENT COMPLETED DURING THE CLASS. The assessment task for the Nernst Potential practical is completed in small groups (2-3 students) during your allocated class, and handed in by the end of the session. It is not possible to attend another practical if you miss your own session. Please see the Assessment section of the course profile for more details about the assessment and instances of absence, and contact the SBMS Student and Assessment Administration team (sbms@enquire.uq.edu.au) or the Laboratory Coordinator (j.kibedi@uq.edu.au) for advice regarding your individual circumstance

: Cardiac Function Series - Class 1 Throughout the Cardiac Function practical series, you will work within a team of fellow students to develop and conduct a hypothesis-driven experiment examining the contractility of the heart under various conditions, using an animal (cane toad) model. This experience will introduce you to the foundations of scientific inquiry, from ideation and execution, through to analysis, interpretation and communication of findings, culminating in submission of a formal scientific report. You will be guided in all aspects of this semester-long learning process through resources, scaffolded activities and the mentorship of a tutor dedicated to your team. The series is designed to introduce and foster your understanding of scientific inquiry & communication, and the development of transferrable skills that many of you will draw on in other courses, or as postgraduate professionals and/or students (e.g. in a MD program). CF Class 1 will focus on your team formation, narrowing down the focus of your project, seeking out and identifying some relevant literature, and a mud-map of your experimental approach. This session will also begin to introduce you to conventions in scientific writing. Throughout the CF practical series, you are encouraged to think ahead and work on tasks relevant to your ultimate completion of the scientific report, and take advantage of the time available with your tutor to work through your ideas and questions about data analysis, finding and understanding literature, and improving your understanding of the final writing task. Please note, it is not possible to attend a different practical Group to your own. If you miss a session in the CF series, there are a number of things you can do to catch up on what you missed. These include things like reaching out to your team members directly and/or wider BIOM2011 cohort via the course Ed Discussion page, reviewing the relevant resources associated with that class to complete any tasks that are possible to do so outside of class (e.g., researching literature), and preparing questions to ask your team/tutor in the next session. If you miss either/both of the experimental sessions , please liaise with your team members or the wider BIOM2011 cohort via Ed Discussion page to obtain data generated from your experiments

: Cardiac Function Series - Class 2 See 'Cardiac Function Series - Class 1' for an overview and objectives of this practical series in BIOM2011. CF Class 2 will be the first of two experimental sessions available to you to conduct your experiments. Class 2 is an opportunity to implement the experimental design your team has developed, and fine-tune or troubleshoot any aspects that require further consideration for rigor and reliability before running your experiment again (if you wish/need to) in CF Class 3. Throughout the CF practical series, you are encouraged to think ahead and work on tasks relevant to your ultimate completion of the scientific report, and take advantage of the time available with your tutor to work through your ideas and questions about data analysis, finding and understanding literature, and improving your understanding of the final writing task. Please note, it is not possible to attend a different practical Group to your own. If you miss a session in the CF series, there are a number of things you can do to catch up on what you missed. search_blob: BIOM2011 Integrative Cell & Tissue Biology | Students will develop an understanding of how cells associate & interact to fulfill their normal functions in tissues & organs of the human body. This will include the basic principles of integration & regulation that underpin normal tissue biology. BIOM2011 is integrative cell and tissue biology . The course will focus on the structure of cells and cell membranes and the functional importance of this. In particular, fundamental aspects of how cells respond to the environment, including neighbouring cells and the extracellular environment will be covered. This includes ion channels in the membranes and ionic movements across these membranes and the control of cell and tissue function in response to changes in channel states and different conditions in the cell's environment. | MODULE 1: Muscle Cells [Launikonis] including Course Introduction [Launikonis & Kibedi] At the start of Lecture 1, Dr Brad and Dr Judit will provide a brief overview of the course aims, assessment design and other information relevant to your successful engagement and learning in BIOM2011. After this introduction, Dr Brad will commence delivering the Muscle Cells module of content. WEEK 1: L1 & L2 - Intro, Muscle Cells 1 & 2 L 3 - Muscle Cells 3 WEEK 2: L4 & 5 - Muscle Cells 4 & 5 L6 - Revision lecture : Nernst Potential Determination of the relationship between [K+] and membrane potential and determination of unknown [K+]. In this practical, you will construct a standard curve to fit the relationship between known [K+] concentration on either side of an artificial membrane and the electrical potential set across it. You will be supplied with an "unknown" solution, and will have to determine its [K+] from the curve you constructed. You will also prepare a standard physiological solution and determine the accuracy of your solution based on the membrane potential you record. THIS PRACTICAL HAS ASSESSMENT COMPLETED DURING THE CLASS. The assessment task for the Nernst Potential practical is completed in small groups (2-3 students) during your allocated class, and handed in by the end of the session. It is not possible to attend another practical if you miss your own session. Please see the Assessment section of the course profile for more details about the assessment and instances of absence, and contact the SBMS Student and Assessment Administration team (sbms@enquire.uq.edu.au) or the Laboratory Coordinator (j.kibedi@uq.edu.au) for advice regarding your individual circumstance MODULE 2: Sensory Systems [Coakley] Dr Sean Coakley will deliver 6 lectures for Module 2, Sensory Systems. This experience will introduce you to the foundations of scientific inquiry, from ideation and execution, through to analysis, interpretation and communication of findings, culminating in submission of a formal scientific report. You will be guided in all aspects of this semester-long learning process through resources, scaffolded activities and the mentorship of a tutor dedicated to your team. The series is designed to introduce and foster your understanding of scientific inquiry & communication, and the development of transferrable skills that many of you will draw on in other courses, or as postgraduate professionals and/or students (e.g. in a MD program). title: Advanced Integrative Cell & Tissue Biology
prerequisite_raw: BIOL1040 or BIOL1050 or (BIOM1001 and BIOM1002)
description: The course will provide essential information as to how cells work together in tissue and organ function. The practical classes employ small-group learning and advanced methods to work on projects that are directly relevant to current research. BIOM2013 is integrative cell and tissue physiology for Advanced Sciences . The course will focus on the structure of cells and cell membranes and the functional importance of this . In particular, fundamental aspects of how cells respond to the environment, including neighbouring cells and the extracellular environment will be covered. This includes ion channels in the membranes and ionic movements across these membranes and the control of cell function in response to changes in channel states and different conditions in the cell's environment.
learning_outcomes: Explain the cellular and molecular processes that lead to coordinated and regulated activity in tissues of the body
Recognise and explain the basic role of the nervous system and endocrine systems as effectors in maintaining homeostasis
Describe the specialization of cell structures and how this relates to function
Explain how changes at the cellular level meet the demand of the host tissue
Demonstrate competency and creativity in experimental design and scientific reasoning
Participate in, and explain the experimental process and role of model systems in discovery of new knowledge

: Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) Series - Class 1 Throughout the Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) practical series, you will work within a team of fellow students to develop and conduct a hypothesis-driven experiment examining mechanoreceptor function in the woodroach leg. To do this, you will be introduced to measuring extracellular activity via an electrophysiology (ePhys) technique, which requires some practice and care to master. These exercises are aimed at introducing you to the uncertainty and challenges of research, whereby resilience is needed to troubleshoot and overcome challenges in experiments. This experience will introduce you to the foundations of scientific inquiry, from ideation and execution, through to analysis, interpretation and communication of findings, culminating in submission of a formal scientific report. You will be guided in all aspects of this semester-long learning process through resources, scaffolded activities and the mentorship of a tutor dedicated to your team. The series is designed to introduce and foster your understanding of scientific inquiry & communication, and the development of transferrable skills that many of you will draw on in other courses, or as postgraduate professionals and/or students (e.g. in a MD program). MF (ePhys) Class 1 will focus on your team formation, narrowing down the focus of your project, seeking out and identifying some relevant literature, and a mud-map of your experimental approach. This session will also begin to introduce you to conventions in scientific writing. Throughout the MF (ePhys) series, you are encouraged to think ahead and work on tasks relevant to your ultimate completion of the scientific report, and take advantage of the time available with your tutor to work through your ideas and questions about data analysis, finding and understanding literature, and improving your understanding of the final writing task. Please note, it is not possible to 'make up' for missing a practical class. If you miss a session in the ePhys series, there are a number of things you can do to catch up on what you missed. These include things like reaching out to your team members directly and/or wider BIOM2013 cohort via the course Ed Discussion page, reviewing the relevant resources associated with that class to complete any tasks that are possible to do so outside of class (e.g., researching literature), and preparing questions to ask your team/tutor in the next session. If you miss either of the experimental sessions , please liaise with your team members or the wider BIOM2013 cohort via Ed Discussion page to obtain data generated from your experiments

search_blob: BIOM2013 Advanced Integrative Cell & Tissue Biology | The course will provide essential information as to how cells work together in tissue and organ function. The practical classes employ small-group learning and advanced methods to work on projects that are directly relevant to current research. BIOM2013 is integrative cell and tissue physiology for Advanced Sciences . The course will focus on the structure of cells and cell membranes and the functional importance of this . In particular, fundamental aspects of how cells respond to the environment, including neighbouring cells and the extracellular environment will be covered. This includes ion channels in the membranes and ionic movements across these membranes and the control of cell function in response to changes in channel states and different conditions in the cell's environment. | MODULE 1: Muscle Cells [Launikonis] including Course Introduction [Launikonis & Kibedi] At the start of Lecture 1, Dr Brad and Dr Judit will provide a brief overview of the course aims, assessment design and other information relevant to your successful engagement and learning in BIOM2011. After this introduction, Dr Brad will commence delivering the Muscle Cells module of content. WEEK 1: L1 & L2 - Intro, Muscle Cells 1 & 2 L 3 - Muscle Cells 3 WEEK 2: L4 & 5 - Muscle Cells 4 & 5 L6 - Revision lecture : Nernst Potential Determination of the relationship between [K+] and membrane potential and determination of unknown [K+]. In this practical, you will construct a standard curve to fit the relationship between known [K+] concentration on either side of an artificial membrane and the electrical potential set across it. You will be supplied with an "unknown" solution, and will have to determine its [K+] from the curve you constructed. You will also prepare a standard physiological solution and determine the accuracy of your solution based on the membrane potential you record. THIS PRACTICAL HAS ASSESSMENT COMPLETED DURING THE CLASS. The assessment task for the Nernst Potential practical is completed in small groups (2-3 students) during your allocated class, and handed in by the end of the session. It is not possible to attend another practical if you miss your own session. Please see the Assessment section of the course profile for more details about the assessment and instances of absence, and contact the SBMS Student and Assessment Administration team (sbms@enquire.uq.edu.au) or the Laboratory Coordinator (j.kibedi@uq.edu.au) for advice regarding your individual circumstance MODULE 2: Sensory Systems [Coakley] Dr Sean Coakley will deliver 6 lectures for Module 2, Sensory Systems. WEEK 3: L1-3 - Sensory 1, 2 & 3 WEEK 4: L4 & 5 - Sensory 4 & 5 L6 - Revision lecture : Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) Series - Class 1 Throughout the Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) practical series, you will work within a team of fellow students to develop and conduct a hypothesis-driven experiment examining mechanoreceptor function in the woodroach leg. To do this, you will be introduced to measuring extracellular activity via an electrophysiology (ePhys) technique, which requires some practice and care to master. These exercises are aimed at introducing you to the uncertainty and challenges of research, whereby resilience is needed to troubleshoot and overcome challenges in experiments. This experience will introduce you to the foundations of scientific inquiry, from ideation and execution, through to analysis, interpretation and communication of findings, culminating in submission of a formal scientific report. You will be guided in all aspects of this semester-long learning process through resources, scaffolded activities and the mentorship of a tutor dedicated to your team. The series is designed to introduce and foster your understanding of scientific inquiry & communication, and the development of transferrable skills that many of you will draw on in other courses, or as postgraduate professionals and/or students (e.g. in a MD program). MF (ePhys) Class 1 will focus on your team formation, narrowing down the focus of your project, seeking out and identifying some relevant literature, and a mud-map of your experimental approach. This session will also begin to introduce you to conventions in scientific writing. Throughout the MF (ePhys) series, you are encouraged to think ahead and work on tasks relevant to your ultimate completion of the scientific report, and take advantage of the time available with your tutor to work through your ideas and questions about data analysis, finding and understanding literature, and improving your understanding of the final writing task. Please note, it is not possible to 'make up' for missing a practical class. If you miss a session in the ePhys series, there are a number of things you can do to catch up on what you missed. These include things like reaching out to your team members directly and/or wider BIOM2013 cohort via the course Ed Discussion page, reviewing the relevant resources associated with that class to complete any tasks that are possible to do so outside of class (e.g., researching literature), and preparing questions to ask your team/tutor in the next session. If you miss either of the experimental sessions , please liaise with your team members or the wider BIOM2013 cohort via Ed Discussion page to obtain data generated from your experiments : Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) Series - Class 2 See 'Mechanoreceptor Function (ePhys) Series - Class 1' for an overview and objectives of this practical series in BIOM2013. title: Integrated Physiology & Pharmacology for Dentistry
description: BIOM2052 (Integrated Physiology & Pharmacology for Dentistry) extends from the anatomy & physiology learnt in BIOM1052. This knowledge then forms the basis for understanding pharmacology, with relevance to dentistry where possible The course revisits physiology of several body systems, but major emphasis is placed on understanding general principles of pharmacology as well as drugs relevant to various body systems. BIOM2052 ﾠprovides an introduction to general pharmacology and then leads into systems pharmacology, with relevance to dentistry where applicable.ﾠ
learning_outcomes: Explain the essential biological basis governing normal processes in the body (physiology).
Describe the basis of pharmacology and the principles of treatment of various body system disorders.
Explain how pharmacological principles and drug treatments apply to dentistry.
Describe how physiological and pharmacological experiments are conducted in the laboratory.
Integrate the concepts of physiology and pharmacology to a body system, in health or disease.

title: Human Anatomy
prerequisite_raw: BIOL1040 or BIOL1050 or (BIOM1001 and BIOM1002) or (BIOM1070 and BIOM1071)
description: This course provides an introduction to human gross anatomy. Using prosected anatomical resources, students will learn macroscopic structure of human organ systems including the musculoskeletal, nervous and visceral systems. This course is pertinent to higher level studies in the biological sciences and is a prerequisite for 3rd year dissection and research courses in human anatomy. BIOM2020 aims to provide an understanding of the macroscopic structure of the human body. This goal will be obtained using a combination of lectures and prosected cadavers in the UQ Gross Anatomy Facility (GAF). You will learn: the language of anatomy; basic structure and function of major anatomical systems of the body; how to identify and describe superficial and deep body structures in situ;ﾠgain an appreciation of ethical matters surrounding the study of human material; and how to handle human remains in a mortuary setting.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate a comprehensive introductory-level understanding of human body structure at the macroscopic level, using correct anatomical terminology (all body systems, except integumentary system).
Demonstrate an awareness of the body donation program and the legal, ethical, and Health Safety and Wellness (HSW) matters around the study of donated human bodies.
Recognise and identify specific anatomical structures in situ and in isolated specimens
Infer function of a given structure from anatomical principles
Effectively communicate anatomical concepts to colleagues using discipline specific language.
Correctly and safely conduct examinations in a human anatomy laboratory.

Demonstrate an awareness of the body donation program and the legal, ethical, and Health Safety and Wellness (HSW) matters around the study of donated human bodies. title: Integrated Biomedical Sciences for Midwifery

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Practical/ Demonstration, Quiz

assessments.task: Fact Sheet on one maternal disorder

description: In this course, Bachelor of midwifery students and dual degree students will gain an integrated understanding of how different physiological systems must adapt to allow initiation, continuation, and completion of a healthy pregnancy. As most pregnancy induced anatomical and physiological adaptations involve the endocrine system, this will feature heavily in this course. This course also describes in further details, the development of the fetus and newborn and the importance of using the correct pharmaceuticals in pregnancy to minimise harm to the fetus while allowing for appropriate treatment of the mother. This course also highlights the pathological processes which mediate adverse pregnancy outcomes.
learning_outcomes: Describe the structure and function of the endocrine system and pregnancy related disorders that may be related to endocrine dysfunction.
Identify the various pathologies associated with structures and functions of endocrine, cardiovascular, nervous and gastrointestinal systems

Examine the physiological changes associated with the nervous, endocrine, cardiovascular and gastrointestinal systems during pregnancy. Also demonstrate an understanding of how disruption of processes related to these systems may lead to pregnancy disorders.
Understand anatomical and physiological roles of the placenta during pregnancy
Recognise and apply the principles that determine the quality use of medicines to patient therapy in healthcare
Demonstrate competence in the therapeutic use of drugs, drug safety and the use of drugs in their professional fields
Explain the pharmacology and therapeutics relevant to drug use during pregnancy and birth
Apply basic observational and experimental techniques in human physiology
Demonstrate an understanding of common congenital disorders and adverse birth outcomes
Understand the anatomical and physiological adaptations of the Puerperium
topics: series- Biomedical Adaptations in pregnancy [Cuffe] Module 1: Biomedical Adaptations in pregnancy This module will provide you with a solid understanding of the anatomical, physiological, metabolic, endocrine and immune changes that occur throughout pregnancy, how all other systems in the body are changed by pregnancy and how pregnancy complications such as gestational diabetes might develop, also include lectures focused on therapeutics in pregnancy
Introduction to the course [Cuffe]

series- Cardiovascular development in pregnancy and lactation [Cuffe], [Clifton], [Isaacs] Module 2: Cardiovascular development in pregnancy and lactation This module will build on your current anatomical and physiological knowledge of the cardiovascular system and focus on complications that relate to this system. This module will also include lectures related to breast development and lactation

series- Gastrointestinal disorders, depression and asthma in pregnancy [Clifton] Module 3: Gastrointestinal disorders in pregnancy, depression and Asthma This module will include GIT disorders of pregnancy as well as asthma and depression
series- Pain, Headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy [Borges] Module 4: Headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy This module will include lectures on headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy

series- Fetal and New-born development [Cuffe] Module 5: Fetal and New-born development This module will build on what you learnt in first year about embryonic development and focus on common challenges and complications related to the fetus. This module will also focus on the extreme physiological adaptations the the new-born undergoes to transition to the external environment
Revision and exam prep lecture

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 3 (23 Feb - 15 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: series- Biomedical Adaptations in pregnancy [Cuffe] Module 1: Biomedical Adaptations in pregnancy This module will provide you with a solid understanding of the anatomical, physiological, metabolic, endocrine and immune changes that occur throughout pregnancy, how all other systems in the body are changed by pregnancy and how pregnancy complications such as gestational diabetes might develop, also include lectures focused on therapeutics in pregnancy
Introduction to the course [Cuffe]

Anatomical changes in pregnancy & pathology[Cuffe] This practical class will focus on making sure students understand how the body changes during pregnancy. This class will also include specimens allowing students to learn more about key pathologies that can occur related to pregnancy

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus practical[Cuffe] Students will be asked to fast before coming to class before they measure blood glucose before and after consuming a glucose load. This practical class involves students undertaking a modified version of the OGTT that all pregnant woman undergo. Students will learn how this process detects GDM and why glucose control is disrupted in pregnancy

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 4 To Week 7 (16 Mar - 19 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: series- Cardiovascular development in pregnancy and lactation [Cuffe], [Clifton], [Isaacs] Module 2: Cardiovascular development in pregnancy and lactation This module will build on your current anatomical and physiological knowledge of the cardiovascular system and focus on complications that relate to this system. This module will also include lectures related to breast development and lactation

Postpartum haemorrhage [Clifton] This face to face workshop will focus on blood loss and hypovolemia related to childbirth. Following an introduction on the topic, students will work through a series of questions related to current Australian guidelines with some guidance provided. Discussion around clinical experiences will be included

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 8 To Week 9 (20 Apr - 03 May)
learning_activities.topic: series- Gastrointestinal disorders, depression and asthma in pregnancy [Clifton] Module 3: Gastrointestinal disorders in pregnancy, depression and Asthma This module will include GIT disorders of pregnancy as well as asthma and depression

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 10 To Week 11 (04 May - 17 May)
learning_activities.topic: series- Pain, Headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy [Borges] Module 4: Headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy This module will include lectures on headaches and epilepsy in pregnancy

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 11 To Week 13 (11 May - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: series- Fetal and New-born development [Cuffe] Module 5: Fetal and New-born development This module will build on what you learnt in first year about embryonic development and focus on common challenges and complications related to the fetus. This module will also focus on the extreme physiological adaptations the the new-born undergoes to transition to the external environment

Pain physiology in labour [Borges] This workshop will focus on understanding different types of pain and how medications are used to manage pain. This workshop will include a range of principles that apply to all types of pain but will also include concepts specific to pain management in labour

Integration workshop (Cuffe) This face to face workshop will bring together many of the ideas learned throughout the course and focus on understanding what goes wrong in pregnancy when complications do occur. This workshop will include a number of activities relating various symptoms to the pathophysiology underlying conditions that occur throughout pregnancy
Revision and exam prep lecture
