title: Biologics

assessments.task: TBL Workshop Session Blackboard Quiz Online

description: Biologics include a wide range of products that include vaccines, blood and blood components, somatic cells, gene therapy, tissues and tissue-engineered products, and recombinant therapeutic proteins. The course will cover a variety of topics associated with the discovery, development, characterisation and production of biologics as human therapeutics, focussing predominantly on recombinant therapeutic proteins. The course will provide an in-depth coverage of the technologies and bioprocesses associated with biologics production. The course will also have a major focus on the global biologics industry and its continued evolution. This course is designed to give students an in-depth appreciation of the many facets of biologics including definitions, discovery, classes of biologics,ﾠtherapeutic modalities, fermentation processes, downstream processing, process economics, regulatory considerations and Good Manufacturing Processes (GMP).ﾠAn analysisﾠof enabling technologies is also given to demonstrate the many disciplines behind the recent spectacular increase in the number of biologics approved for treatment of human, animal, and plant diseases.
learning_outcomes: Competently appraise the global biologics industry and future opportunities.
Explain the enabling technologies underpinning the research and development of biologics.
Discuss in detail the classes of biologics, therapeutic modalities, production and purification processes, process economics and analytical testing of biologic preparations.
Critically assess and appraise relevant literature in biologics and draw parallels between biologicals and conventional pharmaceuticals.

The course will cover a variety of topics associated with the discovery, development, characterisation and production of biologics as human therapeutics, focussing predominantly on recombinant therapeutic proteins. The course will provide an in-depth coverage of the technologies and bioprocesses associated with biologics production. The course will also have a major focus on the global biologics industry and its continued evolution. This course is designed to give students an in-depth appreciation of the many facets of biologics including definitions, discovery, classes of biologics,ﾠtherapeutic modalities, fermentation processes, downstream processing, process economics, regulatory considerations and Good Manufacturing Processes (GMP).ﾠAn analysisﾠof enabling technologies is also given to demonstrate the many disciplines behind the recent spectacular increase in the number of biologics approved for treatment of human, animal, and plant diseases. | Quiz Blackboard quiz during TBL Optional site visit Site visit to Biomanufacturing facility series Please refer to the Course timetable available on the Blackboard site for detailed information Team based learning Revision and critical analysis of lecture material. Please refer to the Course timetable available on the Blackboard site for detailed information, and section 4.2 below | Competently appraise the global biologics industry and future opportunities. Explain the enabling technologies underpinning the research and development of biologics. Discuss in detail the classes of biologics, therapeutic modalities, production and purification processes, process economics and analytical testing of biologic preparations. Critically assess and appraise relevant literature in biologics and draw parallels between biologicals and conventional pharmaceuticals.

title: Honours Research Project
description: Biotechnology Honours students will undertake their own original research project under the guidance of an academic supervisor - based at any of UQs Faculties or Institutes. Students will learn and be assessed on their scientific communication skills in both oral and written presentations of their research project. In BIOT4661, you will undertake a research project in an area of biotechnology of your own choosing and interest – this can span the full spectrum of biotechnology research including basic research, translational research, clinical research and commercialization. You will conduct your research through a long placement in a world-class research environment under supervision of an academic researcher. You will develop your ability to critically analyse the scientific literature, and to conceive, design, and conduct experiments independently. You will develop your skills to assess and articulate the significance of your scholarly work, and to communicate your scientific findings through written submissions and oral presentations.
learning_outcomes: Contribute to the advancement of relevant scientific fields through original research
Critically evaluate existing research and identify gaps in knowledge relevant to your project

Conduct an independent research project including: documenting project progress, generating appropriate evidence through a reproducible method, objectively analysing data, developing evidence-based arguments to support conclusions, and critically evaluating the impact and limitations your research
Explain your ideas effectively to a general audience by using effective audio-visual means
Report the rationale and findings in a written document

learning_activities.topic: Proposal seminar Present a 15 minute seminar outlining the area of the project and the proposed research to be undertaken, with 5 minutes allowed for questions

Final Research Seminar One week after submitting the Research Report, present a seminar describing the Research Project and outcomes. The seminar will cover a 25 minute period: 15 minutes presentation plus 10 minutes question and answer time
Lab performance Practical or theoretical experimental work, under the guidance of an experienced supervisor

Lab notebook Recording the planning and outcome of experiments in a manner suitable for scientific research. You will report the results via discussion forums and/or group presentations within the lab, in your Mid-year Methods Write-up and Check-point, and in your final Research Report

title: Quality Management Systems in Biotechnology

assessments.task: GLP Study Plan (Group-Based)  Team or group-based
GCP Clinical Protocol (Group-Based)  Team or group-based

description: This course will provide students with a comprehensive introduction to the shared principles of all quality management systems used within the biotechnology sector and beyond. It will then focus on critical quality standards encountered along the pathway to commercialisation for biotechnology / pharmaceutical products. Biotechnology involves the use of biology to develop new products, methods and organisms intended to improve human health and society. It is multidisciplinary and is applied in areas as diverse as healthcare, agriculture, industry and the environment. Biotechnology products have the potential to directly impact human health and so it is absolutely critical to ensure the safety of these products. This is typically achieved through the application of strict regulatory requirements, overseen by relevant government authorities.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate understanding of the shared principles of all quality management systems.
Develop comprehensive knowledge of critical quality standards which may be encountered when taking a biotechnology product to market.
Facilitate the conduct of a GLP study and confidently operate within a GLP environment.
Facilitate the conduct of a GCP study and confidently operate within a GCP environment.
Critique the requirements of a facility operating under GMP.
Recognise the importance of teamwork in a professional environment and develop the skills and attributes necessary to successfully operate as part of a team.
Appraise the way in which GMOs are controlled in Australia.
topics: Part 2: General Quality Management Systems (Lectures, Weeks 1-5) Part 2: General Quality Management Systems (Modules 01-09) Lectures = Simon Lake Part 2 comprises nine General Quality Management System modules. They are as follows: 1) Risk Management 2) Quality System Documentation 3) Organisational Structure & Training 4) Data Integrity 5) Audits, Auditors & Auditing 6) Nonconformities, CAPA & Improvement 7) Facilities & Equipment 8) Computerised Systems & IT Infrastructure 9) Retention of Records & Materials / Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery Note: 2 Modules per week (except Week 1 which will have 1 module)

Part 1: Course Introduction (Lecture, Week 1) Part 1: Course Introduction Lecture = Simon Lake An overarching introduction to the course will be provided to students. This will provide an excellent opportunity to clarify course details, highlight critical dates and outline expectations. It will also allow any initial student questions to be addressed

Part 3: Key Quality Standards (Lectures, Weeks 6-12) Part 3: Key Quality Standards Lectures = Simon Lake (GLP, GCP & ISO 17025), Dianne Jackson-Matthews (GMP), Charles Ross (GMP), TBD (ISO 13485) Part 3 comprises: 1) Good Manufacturing Practice (Weeks 6 & 7) 2) Good Laboratory Practice (Weeks 8 & 9) 3) Good Clinical Practice (Weeks 10 & 11) 4) ISO 17025 (Week 12) 5) ISO 13485 (Week 12)

Part 4: GMOs / OGTR, Accreditation vs Certification (Lecture, Week 13) Part 4: GMOs / OGTR, Accreditation vs Certification Lecture = Simon Lake In this section, students will be introduced to the governance relating to Genetically Modified Organisms (GMOs) and the role of the Office of Gene Technology (OGTR) in regulating GMOs. Students will also be provided with a brief look at the key differences between accreditation and certification and other international standards they may encounter within a biotechnology context (e.g. ISO 13485 – Medical Devices)

learning_activities.topic: Part 5: Site Visits (Final Dates to be Confirmed) Part 5: Site Visits Excursions = Simon Lake Internal students will have the opportunity to visit organisations working under various quality management systems. Further information on dates will be provided via Blackboard

title: Current Innovations in Agricultural Biotechnology

assessments.task: Case Study Written Assignment - End Semester

description: This course will provide a broad understanding of the applications of biotechnologies in agriculture including livestock, crops and horticulture. We will also cover, in detail, specific enabling technologies that have allowed translational changes in many agricultural industries including genomics, genome editing, genomic selection, proteomics, recombinant DNA technology, bioinformatics, and molecular diagnostics. Using these enabling technologies, we will describe the most recent advances in plant and livestock pathogen diagnostics, speed crop breeding, crop and livestock nutrition, livestock reproductive biology, vaccinomics for livestock health, genetics and breeding, crop and livestock gene editing, and livestock welfare initiatives. Students will learn about the current global trends in agriculture and how cutting edge biotechnological tools are changing industries to meet food production demands. The course will feature each week different University of Queensland Senior researcher world-renowned in their indicative fields of research. Have you ever wondered how cutting‑edge biotechnology is reshaping the way we grow food, protect livestock, and sustain our planet? Or how tools such as genome editing, bioinformatics, and molecular biology are driving the next revolution in agriculture?
learning_outcomes: Describe and explain global agricultural trends in terms of innovation
Assess and examine current and future opportunities for applications of biotechnology to agriculture
Demonstrate an advanced understanding of biotechnological developments in livestock industry, crop production and horticulture
Critically review and evaluate relevant literature of novel agricultural biotechnological developments
Design potential biotechnology solutions to challenges in agriculture

title: Honours Coursework Topics in Molecular Biosciences

assessments.task: Participation in discussion of Experimental Techniques

description: This one-semester course is the coursework component of the BSc(Hons) and BAdvSc(Hons) program for students undertaking an Honours year in the molecular biosciences through the School of Chemistry and Molecular Biosciences. Learning material goes beyond the content taught in first second and third level courses in these disciplines. The course will be delivered in the form of lectures, seminars, and workshops. It is assumed that you have completed a plan in a relevant discipline; have a sound working knowledge of the major technical areas of that discipline, and a desire to develop your knowledge of one of these in more depth; possess a curiosity for discovery through scholarly research; have good written and oral communication skills, and the ability to use contemporary software tools to undertake analysis and to prepare reports; and are able to manage your time effectively, to set priorities, to meet deadlines and to act in an ethical and professional manner.
learning_outcomes: Apply advanced knowledge and problem-solving skills in your area of specialisation (Biochemistry, Molecular Biology, Microbiology or Genetics).
Apply advanced knowledge of research techniques relevant to aspects of your research work.
Communicate advanced knowledge of research techniques relevant in your area of specialisation.

title: Plant Biology & Biotechnology
description: One of the grand challenges facing the world is tailoring our plants and crops to meet the demands of the next and future generations, including ensuring ongoing food security and sustainable food production amid changing environments. This course will engage students in active learning about concepts in plant genetics, molecular biology and physiology to understand developmental processes at the core of plant form and function. Students will have hands-on experience using cutting-edge biotechnology methods and gain real-world practical skills required to engineer and adapt plants for our future needs. These skills underpin both fundamental plant science research and also modern plant breeding and agriculture. One of the grand challenges facing the world is tailoring our plants and crops to meet the demands of the next and future generations while minimising the impacts of agricultural systems in the environment. This task is made more difficult by the acceleration in climate change experienced in recent years, with extreme weather events becoming the norm rather than the exception. Ensuring food security and sustainable food production amid changing environments will require a new generation of science leaders with multidisciplinary expertise in plant biology, biotechnology and commercial entrepreneurship.
learning_outcomes: Evaluate key concepts and approaches in plant molecular biology including gene structure, genetic transformation, gene cloning strategies, transgene expression strategies, gene silencing, regulatory and commercial issues in plant biotechnology.
Evaluate and review concepts in plant growth and development.

Design, create, analyse and report on scientific experiments using core techniques in this field ranging from gene transfer to the biochemical and molecular analysis of plants and develop additional skills for career advancement in plant biology and biotechnology.
Understand how to apply key biotechnical techniques in the laboratory and interpret results obtained from experiments, including plant transformation, cloning and genomic analysis.
Critically evaluate claims for benefits and/or risks of plant gene technologies and reach informed judgements about their merits in a global context.
Formulate the link between plant biotechnology theory and commercial applications using an evidence-based approach.

What is in a plant genome (Crisp) Plant genomes I - What is in a plant genome (Crisp) Plant genomes II - Genome expansion (Crisp) Plant genomes III - Polyploidy (Crisp) Gene regulation I - Gene regulatory elements and biotech (Crisp) Tutorial - Gene regulation and functional genomics (Crisp) Gene regulation II - Chromatin (Crisp) Gene regulation III - Epigenetics (Crisp) Tutorial - Epigenome editing and intro to preprints (Crisp) Exam preparation (Crisp) Schedule A detailed list of lectures and practicals can be found in the blackboard site for this course. Sub-activity: Prac Module 1 - Gene Transfer, CRISPR/Cas9 and Analysis of Transgenic Plant Lines - laboratory - 4 weeks (Massel/Crisp) Prac Module 2 - Practical gene cloning and CRISPR design - computer labs (Crisp/Massel/Botella) - 2 weeks schedule To provide a link to commercial applications of fundamental concepts, students will undertake evidence-based reviews of case studies on the commercialisation of plant biotechnology during a tutorial series | Evaluate key concepts and approaches in plant molecular biology including gene structure, genetic transformation, gene cloning strategies, transgene expression strategies, gene silencing, regulatory and commercial issues in plant biotechnology. Evaluate and review concepts in plant growth and development. Design, create, analyse and report on scientific experiments using core techniques in this field ranging from gene transfer to the biochemical and molecular analysis of plants and develop additional skills for career advancement in plant biology and biotechnology. Understand how to apply key biotechnical techniques in the laboratory and interpret results obtained from experiments, including plant transformation, cloning and genomic analysis. Critically evaluate claims for benefits and/or risks of plant gene technologies and reach informed judgements about their merits in a global context. Formulate the link between plant biotechnology theory and commercial applications using an evidence-based approach.

title: Biotechnology Intellectual Property Case Studies

assessments.task: Examination of a trade secret litigation case
Deconstruction and analysis of a dispute
Myriad decisions in the U.S. and Australia

description: Study of cases in intellectual property (IP) in biotechnology. Criteria for patentability. Breadth of claims, reduction to practice, fair basing. Process and product patents. Terms of patent in biotechnology. Differences in patent rules between countries. Plant breeders rights. Prohibitions/exclusions from patentability in biotechnology. Compulsory licensing. Illustrative Case studies in biotechnology IP: The GTG patents, the PCR patents, Taxol patents, Neem patents, Turmeric patents, rice patents, Affymetrix patents, "Southern" patents, EST patents, sequence patents, stem cell patents. Strategies for managing biotechnology IP Analysis of the parts of a patent and a critical examination of a patent in the context of prior art. Preparation of a case study in IP conflict, dispute or debate in biotechnology. Due to a limited number of places available in this course, there will be a selection process for enrolment with places being allocated based on the results from the prerequisite course and merit using the student's overall GPA as the criterion.
learning_outcomes: Describe several strategies for protection and commercialization of biotechnology IP
List criteria that are used to make decisions about biotechnology IP protection and management strategies and apply those criteria to make decisions
Deconstruct and analyse complex disputes in the field of biotechnology IP
List the important parts of a biotechnology patent
Demonstrate an awareness of the different interpretations of prior art in different countries in relation to biotechnology IP
Examine and evaluate trade secret litigation in biotechnology

learning_activities.topic: Regular consultation with lecturer Regular group consultations with course lecturer and other members of class, to discuss progress with assignment tasks. These consultation sessions, will be arranged in week one of the course. Topics covered will include: Advanced trade secrets; Origin of IP; Case Studies; IP Case Law; Obviousness; Strategies for IP Management
Search Background reading Locate, download and read the relevant patents. Email lecturer to discuss downloaded documents

title: Paper Critique

assessments.task: Submission of Digital Object Identifier (DOI) link

description: Critical review of recent literature in area chosen by student in consultation with academic staff member. Not all published literature is of the same standard and some can even be misleading. This course will give you the opportunity to analyse what you read in the scientific literature, to question/interrogate the accuracy and relevance of the data and to present this in the context of the field of study. These skills are the foundation of good scientific practice.ﾠ
learning_outcomes: Critically read the scientific literature and be able to recognise papers of great scientific merit in a defined area;
Evaluate work already done in an area and assess where deficiencies in the knowledge of the subject lie;
Critically review a scientific paper to detect its strengths and weaknesses;
Organise ideas derived from other people's work and write a clear, concise, readily understandable critique in an objective manner.

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Literature Review A short review of a recent peer-reviewed paper on a chosen topic
Paper Critique Contact Weekly contacts (workshop style sessions) to support planning and writing for this Paper Critique. Attendance is voluntary but highly recommended

title: Area Review

assessments.task: Review current literature in your discipline

description: Critical review of recent literature in an area chosen by student in consultation with academic staff member. A literature review examines and evaluates key relevant published work within a particular area of interest. The review will explain current knowledge in the field whilst simultaneously using critical analysis to highlight gaps in knowledge, conflicting or updated theories and possibilities for further exploration. Literature reviews are useful to learn about a new field, update your knowledge, find new avenues to explore and to avoid repeating what has already been done.
learning_outcomes: Critically read the scientific literature and be able to recognise papers of great scientific merit in a defined area;
Comprehensively evaluate work already done in an area and assess where deficiencies in the knowledge of the subject lie;
Critically review an area of research to detect its strengths and weaknesses;
Organise ideas derived from other people's work, and to write a clear, concise, readily understandable review in an objective manner.

title: Research Project B
description: A 2 unit Individual workplace- laboratory- or computer-based project & analysis of a specific topic in bioinformatics, biotechnology, or molecular biology. Projects can be undertaken within university laboratories or in an industry setting with co-supervision by an academic staff member. The project results will be presented via an appropriate report format. Students should enrol in BIOX7002. Students who have completed BIOX7002 may enrol in BIOX7003. Practitioners in the disciplines of Biotechnology, Molecular Biology and Bioinformatics formulate their work around hypotheses, methods, experimentation and data gathering; while in some cases technologies and approaches require development, outcomes require evaluation and data are interpreted. It is difficult to interpret biological data unless one has had first hand experience of gaining reliable data. Of necessity therefore, a professional scientist must acquire experience in the development and application of techniques and experimental procedures, which may include computational techniques and literature research work. This course gives you hands-on experience in planning and executing experiments, tests, and interpreting research results.
learning_outcomes: Plan, execute and interpret experiments accurately
Communicate research results in a written report
Comply with expectations and responsibilities of a professional working environment and engage effectively in the workplace.

The project results will be presented via oral presentation and an appropriate report format. Students completing the course over two semesters commencing in semester 1 and completing in semester 2 enrol in BIOX7011. For all other study combinations refer to the program course list. Practitioners in the disciplines of Biotechnology, Molecular Biology and Bioinformatics formulate their work around hypotheses, methods, experimentation and data gathering; while in some cases technologies and approaches require development, outcomes require evaluation and data are interpreted. It is difficult to interpret biological data unless one has had first hand experience of gaining reliable data. Of necessity therefore, a professional scientist must acquire experience in the development and application of techniques and experimental procedures, which may include computational techniques and literature research work. This course gives you hands-on experience in planning and executing experiments, tests, and interpreting research results. | meeting Laboratory work Performance of original experiments and written communication of results Seminar At the end of the semester a short seminar is to be given to the research group in which you worked | Plan, execute and interpret experiments. Communicate research results in a written report. Communicate research outcomes orally to a small group. Comply with expectations and responsibilities of a professional working environment and engage effectively in the workplace.

title: Literature Review
prerequisite_raw: With permission of the program director. BIOX7101 has a pre-req of BIOX7100. BIOX7100 has no other pre-req.

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: With permission of the program director. BIOX7101 has a pre-req of BIOX7100. BIOX7100 has no other pre-req.

description: Review of literature in a biotechnology area chosen by supervisor or course coordinator with written report in the format of a report for publication. In depth review of literature, in the area of the masters program you are taking, chosen by agreement with supervisor. The review will be in the form of a written report in publication format.
learning_outcomes: Critically read the scientific literature and recognise papers of great scientific merit in a defined area;
Comprehensively evaluate work already done in an area and assess where deficiencies in the knowledge of the subject lie;
Critically review an area of research to detect its strengths and weaknesses;
Organise ideas derived from other people's work, and to write a clear, concise, readily understandable review in an objective manner.

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 3 (23 Feb - 15 Mar)
learning_activities.topic: Literature search Use library and online facilities to locate relevant literature

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Organise and write literature review Map out chapter structure of literature review. Sort literature according to mapped chapters. Draft introduction and conclusions
Compile bibliography Compile bibliography/list of references using Endnote or similar software
Literature Review Contacts Contact sessions (workshop style activity) run weekly to support planning and writing

title: Project Proposal
description: Written proposal in the form of a research grant application based on a research project, recent literature or involving industry partner Applications for funding in science is usually based on a convincing and realistic research proposal. In this course you will select an appropriate research topic and develop a realistic research strategy that addresses important gaps in the literature or which pursues new research directions. You must communicate your ideas clearly and succinctly, and provide a convincing case for the research significance. This course will assist you in developing career-oriented skills such as inductive reasoning via critical analysis of a specific research idea, efficient use of library resources and design of strategies for achieving research goals.ﾠﾠ
learning_outcomes: identify a research problem, and formulate an hypothesis to be tested or knowledge gap to be addressed;
state clearly the aims and significance of the proposed line of research, and prepare a convincing case for its support in a clear, concise, readily understandable and objective manner;

summarise the relevant literature which justifies the importance of the proposed line of research or musters evidence both for and against a proposed hypothesis. Assess this published work and thus evaluate the strengths, weaknesses and likely value of the proposal;

propose an experimental strategy, or direction of research designed to test, establish or disprove any hypothesis and/or address the identified problem. The experiments should be feasible and yield information that will unequivocally achieve the stated aims. Strategies for data analysis should be considered if relevant.

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Preparation of research proposal

title: Industry Research Placement
prerequisite_raw: Completed a minimum of 8 units in a Science postgraduate program (or equivalent).

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: Completed a minimum of 8 units in a Science postgraduate program (or equivalent).

description: This course involves 400-480 (8-unit) hours of industry experience, through a work integrated learning placement within an approved Biotechnology workplace. Students will work with their placement provider to develop and complete a specific project which is both meaningful to the organisation and of sufficient technical content to meet the academic requirements of the course. It enables students to apply, to that industry, their theoretical knowledge and research skills. It is anticipated that students will also acquire an understanding of industry operations and requirements, while also developing and enhancing skills in report writing and project management, thereby enhancing their career opportunities. This course provides students with program-related industry experience and an opportunity to further their professional skills training in an authenticﾠenvironment. Students will gain practical experience in the application of theoretical concepts and knowledge, while acquainting themselves with the real-world conditions and activities of their industry before graduating. Students will undertake 400-480 hours of placementﾠin an approved workplace.
learning_outcomes: Link theory and practice by demonstrating practical processing, research, laboratory or problem-solving skills in a professional work environment.
Demonstrate the ability to effectively communicate orally and in writing with co-workers and/or management.
Demonstrate project management skills including completing an agreed project (or projects) and presenting results within a specified time period.
Demonstrate the ability to work with others and work independently.
Engage actively in critical self-reflection and self-evaluation.

learning_activities.period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Laboratory work Performance of original experiments (or relevant industrial project work) and a written report
meeting Students will meet with their supervisor and discuss the requirements of their project
Seminar At the end of the semester a short seminar is to be given to the research group in which you worked

title: Data Analytics and Information Management

assessments.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Practical/ Demonstration

description: Data Analytics and Information Management concerns the use of data for decision making in business. It focuses on how organisations can create business value from data by modelling, integrating, analysing and visualising data. It helps students develop fundamental data analytics knowledge, experience contemporary data analytic tools and learn about broader organisational and societal implications of data analytics such as data-driven transformation and ethics of big data. Data analytics comprises skills, processes and technologies used to create timely and meaningful insights from organisational data. The insights improve managerial decision-making and help to create significant business value.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of Data Analytics for decision-making.
Apply the principles of data management to model and integrate organisational data.
Apply data analytics techniques as an individual to solve business problems.
Apply data visualisation principles and techniques to represent insights from data.
Demonstrate knowledge of managerial and societal issues related to data.

title: Transforming Business with Information Systems

assessments.category: Notebook/ Logbook, Practical/ Demonstration, Tutorial/ Problem Set

description: As global competition intensifies, organisations that leverage the power of information systems thrive. This course explores the use and management of information systems within organisations, introducing key information systems components such as computer hardware and software, processes, information systems development, implementation and operation in organisations.
learning_outcomes: Explain the terminology and concepts that are the fundamental building blocks of current and emerging business information systems.
Analyse and evaluate the role of business information systems in organisations.
Utilise problem solving strategies to analyse and solve authentic business information system challenges.

title: Fundamental Biomedical Sciences for Nursing

assessments.task: Invigilated End of Semester Examination- Written
Invigilated End of Semester Examination- VIVA

description: BIOM7121 is a biomedical science course for the Master of Nursing Studies program. In this course, you will investigate the pillars of clinical science (anatomy, physiology, pharmacology and pathology) to better understand various body systems. In particular, you will examine the integration of body systems, and the resulting compensatory mechanisms available to the human body to maintain homeostasis. This course aims to provide students with the essential and fundamental knowledge and skills required to identify and comprehend pathophysiological processes linked to various bodily systems, including the:
learning_outcomes: explain the importance of homeostasis in human survival, including the importance of the cellular environment and the cell membrane, cellular communication, and integration of cellular responses
describe the structure of the cells, tissues and organs that constitute some of the human organ systems

identify the principal components, relevant anatomical features and relationships of some of the human organ systems on human cadaveric specimens and communicate effectively using correct anatomical terminology
explain how human body systems maintain health
explain the integration and control of the major body systems

recognise the link between human biology and disease processes and predict some functional deficits that may occur as a result of altered structure, within an ethical framework of respect for both living and deceased humans
identify the causes, risk factors, symptoms and treatment options of common health disorders
explain the mechanisms of actions of common drugs which affect the cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, renal, respiratory, nervous, immune and endocrine systems

learning_activities.topic: During placements, students gain practical, hands-on experience in healthcare settings under the supervision of registered nurses and clinical educators. Students will apply the theoretical knowledge learned in their studies to real-world scenarios, developing essential clinical skills such as patient assessment, administering medications, wound care, and monitoring vital signs. Students also will learn to communicate effectively with patients, families, and healthcare teams while building professional behaviors and confidence. Placements expose students to various clinical environments, such as hospitals, aged care facilities, community health centers, or mental health services, helping them understand the diverse roles of nurses and preparing them for future practice. Further details about your placements are available from you school

Students will apply the theoretical knowledge learned in their studies to real-world scenarios, developing essential clinical skills such as patient assessment, administering medications, wound care, and monitoring vital signs. Students also will learn to communicate effectively with patients, families, and healthcare teams while building professional behaviors and confidence. Placements expose students to various clinical environments, such as hospitals, aged care facilities, community health centers, or mental health services, helping them understand the diverse roles of nurses and preparing them for future practice. title: Machine Learning in Practice
prerequisite_raw: BISM2202 OR 8 units of BInfTech courses

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: BISM2202 OR 8 units of BInfTech courses

description: This course is an advanced offering focused on ways in which organisations can utilise machine learning (ML) in practice to gain insights and make better decisions. Through utilising cutting-edge ML techniques, students will develop analytics skills designed to tackle business challenges. This course is an advanced course concerned with machine learning (ML), which refers to the ways in which enterprises such as businesses, non-profits, and governments can use data to gain insights and make better decisions. With the increasing availability of broad and deep sources of data, ML is becoming an even more critical capability for enterprises of all types and all sizes. The ability to use data effectively to drive timely, precise and profitable decisions has been a critical strategic advantage for companies and is in high demand in the industry.
learning_outcomes: Build your machine learning (ML) capabilities to use data for innovative business solutions.
Enhance knowledge and skills in the current trends in the management and use of ML.
Differentiate, design and assess various ML and data mining models.
Implement efficient ML strategies to solve current business problems.
Identify and translate real-world business problems into ML models.

title: Managing Projects

assessments.task: Project Management Plan and Presentation Team or group-based

description: To ensure successful completion, information systems projects require careful planning, analysis and management. This course introduces students to core project management principles. The course provides students with knowledge and skills in defining project scope, estimating risks, identifying stakeholders, understanding human and organisational factors, project leadership, and project controlling/management. This new course blends hand-on, collaborative workshops sessions with modern online learning materials to explore the essentials of managing information systems projects.
learning_outcomes: Identify key phases of managing information systems projects.
Develop and describe project artefacts including project plans, scheduled, budgets, reports, and dashboards.
Identify and evaluate project related risks and mitigations.
Reflect on human, team, and organisational factors impacting projects.
Work effectively as part of a team to create and communicate best practice project deliverables.

title: Business Information Systems & Analytics Capstone

assessments.task: Emerging Technology: Team presentation  Team or group-based
Digital Transformation Hackathon - Pitch Presentation  Team or group-based
Learning Reflection and Individual Progress Report
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Reflection

description: The ability to manage transformative digital technologies is fundamental for IT managers and entrepreneurs alike. This capstone course enables business information systems students to integrate, expand, and apply their capabilities to the phenomenon of digital transformation. Students will develop analytical skills needed to understand the complexity of real-world management and implementation transformative digital technologies. The course will, in equal parts, examine major concepts and theories related to digital transformation and innovation, while simultaneously provide students the opportunity to apply these concepts and theories through case studies and an innovation hackathon. Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.
learning_outcomes: Define digital transformation and describe how it is enabled by emerging technologies.
Effectively participate in, and manage, business endeavours and initiatives related to digital transformation.
Critically evaluate how technology can be leveraged to improve intra- and inter-organisational processes intended to enhance a firms competitive position.
Develop understanding of, and skills related to, service design approaches needed to develop commercial opportunities through digital transformative technologies.
Assess and evaluate the factors that influence how relevant a given technology may become in the future.
Expand the ability to work in teams, while refining written and oral presentation skills, which are essential to the effective and efficient conduct of IT leadership and management in organisations.

Theories of Adoption Organisational Adoption of Technology Individual Adoption of Technology
Digital Transformation and Ethics Ethics of Data Analytics Managing Digital Privacy
The Innovation Ecosystem Role of Governments in Innovation Role of Customers in Innovation Role of Firms in Innovation Role of Technology in Innovation

Digital Transformation Hackathon - Pt.3 Hackathon mentoring. Because some mentors may be industry-based and/or overseas, they may connect to you via Zoom during class time. You will work with the teaching team to create a near-final solution and prepare for your presentation. Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4th May 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes
Digital Transformation Hackathon - Pt.4 UQ Ventures session 'how to pitch' You will work with the UQ teaching team to finalise your solution and prepare for your presentation
Digital Transformation Hackathon Pt. 5 Final presentations by all teams. Note - depending on class-size, we may create presentation 'streams' across different theatres
Course Review, Q&A and Feedback Hackathon finalists will be given the opportunity to present once more to determine the winning team. Course conclusion and wrap-up

title: Advanced Studies in Business Information Systems
prerequisite_raw: (BISM2207 or BISM3203) + 4 units from the major

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: (BISM2207 or BISM3203) + 4 units from the major

description: This integrative, capstone course is concerned with the interplay of business information systems strategy and organisational policy and strategy making. How business information systems strategy supports the achievement of the strategic ambitions of organisations is a particular focus. Key issues are establishing effective business information strategies and the organisational and environmental precursors to developing such strategies. Emphasis is placed on how business information systems strategy is matched with organisational strategy. Students are expected to show intellectual leadership by planning a significant research project within the domain of business information systems. This integrative, capstone course is concerned with the interplay of business information systems strategy and organisational policy and strategy making. How business information systems strategy supports the achievement of the strategic ambitions of organisations is a particular focus. Key issues are establishing effective business information strategies and the organisational and environmental precursors to developing such strategies. Emphasis is placed on how business information systems strategy is matched with organisational strategy. Students are expected to show intellectual leadership by conducting a significant research project within the domain of business information systems.
learning_outcomes: Describe some major, enduring practical research topics in the information systems discipline.
Develop a practical research question that addresses current business issues based on theoretical and methodological considerations learned in the course.
Undertake practical research in the information systems discipline.
Communicate the practical research project related questions in a clear and compelling way.
Evaluate research papers in the information systems discipline.
Understand and apply the fundamental ethical principles that should underpin the conduct of information systems research.

title: Information Analysis and System Design
prerequisite_raw: BISM2202 OR 12 units of BInfTech or BCompSc courses

description: Structured analysis and design for business; data analysis and design; object-oriented analysis and design; testing and quality assurance; practical group experience with computer-assisted software engineering tools for business applications. This course teaches you how to use design thinking principles to analyse and design business information systems. This course will provide you with the skills and knowledge necessary to effectively design and implement business information systems that meet the needs of organisations and their stakeholders. This course builds your digital skills for many career opportunities, specifically for roles such as a Systems Analyst, Business Analyst, and Project Manager.
learning_outcomes: Describe the major alternative methodologies used in developing information systems and the considerations involved in choosing which methodology to use.
Analyse a business need within a complex environment and develop an appropriate strategy to meet the need, and provide the required information service.
Prepare and use various information gathering techniques for eliciting user information requirements and system expectations.
Construct a variety of system description documents, following agile methodology, including a working prototype.
Explain, collaborate, and innovate through information systems development to address business challenges as an individual and as part of a team.

title: Information Systems for Management

assessments.task: MS Excel Assignment and Reflective Essay

description: Uses of information systems in organisations, management of information systems. Overview of computers and information systems; information systems development, implementation and operation. Practical experience with spreadsheets, database management and other business applications software for business problems. Information systems play a central role in organisations, and they are the lifeblood of any organisation. In your career, your ability to understand, improve, and optimise information systems (including AI-enabled information systems such as Generative AI and agentic AI systems) in organisations and society more broadly is a fundamental skill to possess. The knowledge taught in this course will provide you with the ability to sense, respond, and create change using information systems to build value for your organisation.
learning_outcomes: Apply theoretical knowledge of information systems fundamentals as well as the latest trends in information systems and technology practice in your future careers.

Analyse and critically evaluate the impacts (including ethical and sustainability impacts) information systems (including artificial intelligence) have on individuals, organisations, and society as a whole.
Demonstrate a basic level of knowledge of the different types of information systems such as artificial intelligence and data analytics tools.
Understand the importance of information systems as knowledge, decision and business intelligence tools for managers.

Develop skills to make informed decisions on the development and installation of new information systems and the upgrade of existing information systems, including the development of spreadsheets to support business decision-making.

title: Machine Learning for Analysis
prerequisite_raw: 8 units of MBus or MCom courses and BISM7233 or INFS7233

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: 8 units of MBus or MCom courses and BISM7233 or INFS7233

assessments.task: Jupyter Lifecycle Mastery in ML Project

description: In this course, students will analyse real-world data using advanced Machine Learning (ML) techniques. The course will be hands-on, with an emphasis on technical aspects so that students learn how analysts apply ML to improve business decision-making. This course is an advanced course concerned with machine learning (ML), which refers to the ways in which enterprises such as businesses, non-profits, and governments can use data to gain insights and make better decisions. With the increasing availability of broad and deep sources of data, ML is becoming an even more critical capability for enterprises of all types and all sizes. The ability to use data effectively to drive timely, precise and profitable decisions has been a critical strategic advantage for companies and is in high demand in the industry.
learning_outcomes: Enhance knowledge and skills in the current trends in the management and use of machine learning (ML).
Build your ML capabilities to use data for innovative business solutions.
Differentiate, design, and assess various ML models.
Implement efficient ML strategies to solve current business problems.
Identify and translate real-world business problems into ML models.

Artificial Neural Networks + Quiz 8

title: Information Retrieval and Management

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Role play/ Simulation

description: This course examines data analysis, design, and business issues in relation to database management, conceptual and data modelling, and business applications. Students will gain an overall understanding of the relational database model and learn to design and retrieve information from relational databases using Structured Query Language (SQL). Data plays an integral role in decision-making within modern organisations. Therefore, data needs to be managed efficiently and effectively to enable easy, timely, informative, and accurate access to relevant and useful data. This forms the underlying framework and rationale for the learning content in BISM7206.ﾠ
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate foundational understanding and/or critical application of data modelling techniques to design robust data structures that address complex business requirements.
Apply SQL to extract data from relational information systems, and apply generative AI tools responsibly to support database design, coding and validation.
Explain and apply principles of data quality assurance and apply techniques such as data cleaning, validation, and integrity enforcement to improve the reliability of organisational data.
Apply current Information Systems issues and effectively communicate strategic recommendations for leveraging business data for competitive advantage.
Collaborate effectively in teams and articulate complex information systems concepts through advanced written and oral communication skills.
topics: Course Overview and Intro to DBMS Introduction to database management, including traditional file-based systems, the database management system and fundamental database concepts. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 1, 2)

Entity Relationship Modelling Entity relationship modelling is explored in terms of a data driven approach using entity-relationship diagrams including design elements such as entities, relationships, attributes, keys and multiplicity. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 12 and 13)
Relational Model Relational model concepts are discussed including specific terminology, keys, views and integrity constraints. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 4)
Structured Query Language (I) Introduction to basic SQL DDL and DML statements. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7)
Structured Query Language (II) Exploring aggregation and grouping, and relational operations to enable search conditions and order/grouping of results. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7)
AI Debate Week – No Lecture

Normalisation Explore how normalisation supports database design and how concepts such as partial, transitive, and functional dependencies enable the logical database design to transition and conform to particular normal forms. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 14,15)

Data Quality and Cleaning Data is a valuable resource and access to accurate, current and informative data is critical to business success. Therefore, data needs to be managed and organised like other business assets
Guest Lecture

Transaction Management This lecture explores the critical role of transaction management in business systems, focusing on how organisations maintain data integrity and consistency in multi-user environments and recover from failures to ensure reliable operations. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 22)

Database Security and Control We’ll look at why database security matters, the main threats to data security, and the core ideas of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the CIA triad). We’ll also cover practical countermeasures and finish with an overview of Data Control Language (DCL). Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 20)
Distributed Database Management Systems We examine distributed DBMSs including concepts, problems, technologies and protocols. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 24, 25)
Overview and Big Data & Technologies Exploring why data management technologies and approaches have expanded beyond relational databases and data warehousing

learning_activities.topic: Course Overview and Intro to DBMS Introduction to database management, including traditional file-based systems, the database management system and fundamental database concepts. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 1, 2)

Entity Relationship Modelling Entity relationship modelling is explored in terms of a data driven approach using entity-relationship diagrams including design elements such as entities, relationships, attributes, keys and multiplicity. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 12 and 13)

1: Data & Information; MYSQL prep Students will explore the difference between data and information, examine the 3-level database architecture through discussion and case analysis, and begin preparing for practical database work by setting up and navigating the MySQL environment
Relational Model Relational model concepts are discussed including specific terminology, keys, views and integrity constraints. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 4)

2: Entity Relationship Modelling Students will identify entities and relationships in real-world scenarios, review key concepts such as multiplicity and participation constraints, and apply their understanding through practical ER modelling exercises
Structured Query Language (I) Introduction to basic SQL DDL and DML statements. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7)

3: Relational Modelling Students will work through practical exercises on relations, keys, and integrity constraints, and apply these concepts by creating a relational model and corresponding relational schema
Structured Query Language (II) Exploring aggregation and grouping, and relational operations to enable search conditions and order/grouping of results. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 6,7)

4: SQL Pactical Exercises (1) This tutorial focuses on foundational SQL skills through practical exercises in MySQL, starting with basic queries and manual command-line operations. Students will then engage in AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate, interpret, and refine SQL queries
AI Debate Week – No Lecture

5: In-class AI debate (Assignment 1) This tutorial is dedicated to Assignment 1, where students will participate in an in-class AI debate, critically examining the role of AI in data management and information systems
In-Semester Break

Normalisation Explore how normalisation supports database design and how concepts such as partial, transitive, and functional dependencies enable the logical database design to transition and conform to particular normal forms. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 14,15)

6: SQL practices (II) This tutorial focuses on advancing SQL skills using MySQL Workbench, with an emphasis on tackling complex queries and clarifying challenging concepts. Students will also explore AI-assisted prompt engineering to generate and refine advanced SQL queries, enhancing their problem-solving and query optimization abilities

Data Quality and Cleaning Data is a valuable resource and access to accurate, current and informative data is critical to business success. Therefore, data needs to be managed and organised like other business assets

7: Normalisation exercises Students will engage in practical exercises to identify functional dependencies and apply normalization techniques up to 3NF, reinforcing their understanding of data structure optimization and redundancy reduction
Guest Lecture

8: Assignment Iteration feedback and Data cleaning This tutorial focuses on providing feedback on Assignment 1 iterations and developing data cleaning skills. Students will identify and resolve common data quality issues to prepare datasets for accurate analysis and reporting

Transaction Management This lecture explores the critical role of transaction management in business systems, focusing on how organisations maintain data integrity and consistency in multi-user environments and recover from failures to ensure reliable operations. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 22)

9: Team project This tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables. Labour Day Public Holiday - Monday 4th May 2026 - Check Blackboard for announcements about affected classes

Database Security and Control We’ll look at why database security matters, the main threats to data security, and the core ideas of confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the CIA triad). We’ll also cover practical countermeasures and finish with an overview of Data Control Language (DCL). Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 20)

10: Database Security and team project This tutorial covers practical exercises in database security, focusing on access control and user permissions. Students will also continue working on their team projects
Distributed Database Management Systems We examine distributed DBMSs including concepts, problems, technologies and protocols. Readings/Ref: CB (Ch. 24, 25)
11: Team Project This tutorial is dedicated to the team project, providing students with structured time to work in their groups, develop project deliverables, and receive feedback on their progress
Overview and Big Data & Technologies Exploring why data management technologies and approaches have expanded beyond relational databases and data warehousing
12: Exam consult

title: Business Information Systems Capstone
prerequisite_raw: BISM7233 or 7255 or INFS7211 or 7233 or 7255

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: BISM7233 or 7255 or INFS7211 or 7233 or 7255

assessments.task: Emerging Technology: Team presentation Team or group-based
Digital Transformation Hackathon - Pitch Presentation  Team or group-based
Learning Reflection and Individual Progress Report
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Reflection

description: The ability to manage transformative digital technologies is fundamental for IT managers and entrepreneurs alike. This capstone course enables business information systems students to integrate, expand, and apply their capabilities to the phenomenon of digital transformation. Students will develop analytical skills needed to understand the complexity of real-world management and implementation transformative digital technologies. The course will, in equal parts, examine major concepts and theories related to digital transformation and innovation, while simultaneously provide students the opportunity to apply these concepts and theories through case studies and an innovation hackathon. The group formation method has been updated to minimise the risks of long-term group conflict and short-term confusion, particularly for students who may be unable to attend the first seminar.
learning_outcomes: Define digital transformation and describe how it is enabled by emerging technologies.
Effectively participate in, and manage, business endeavours and initiatives related to digital transformation.
Critically evaluate how technology can be leveraged to improve intra and inter-organisational processes intended to enhance a firms competitive position.
Work in teams to develop an understanding of, and skills related to, service design approaches needed to develop commercial opportunities through digital transformative technologies.
Assess and evaluate the factors that influence how relevant a given technology may become in the future.

title: Business Information Systems Analysis and Design

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: BISM7206 or 2 units COMP/COMS/INFS courses

assessments.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Product/ Design

description: Structured and object-oriented analysis and design of business systems; testing and quality assurance; practical group experience with CASE tools integrated with a relational database for business systems. Critical analysis and discussion of recent research. Systems analysis and design is a process focused on the development of new and the extension of existing information systems. It requires a unique set of problem-solving skills and knowledge of the information systems and business domains – such skills are in high demand in industry.
learning_outcomes: Describe the major alternative methodologies (e.g., agile, lean) that are used in developing information systems and decide which methodology to use in a certain context.
Prepare and use various information-gathering techniques for eliciting user information requirements and system expectations.

Construct and interpret a variety of system description documents, including UML use case, activity, class, sequence, statechart and entity-relationship diagrams, Structured English, as well as screen, form, and report layouts.

Communicate effectively and culturally safe, in both written and oral forms, systems specifications, implementation strategies, and design requirements, and be persuasive and culturally appropriate in these presentations.

Solve and innovate on practical problems that may emerge in the job roles of Business Analyst and/or Systems Analyst using UML integrated environments and low-code platforms as an individual or as part of a team.

title: Information Systems Governance and Assurance

assessments.task: Career Jam: Information Systems Advisory Roles  Team or group-based

description: This course introduces students to the role of information systems governance in IT decision-making. Students will develop skills in using analytic assurance techniques and analysis to develop management recommendations to improve the business's IT capability and to investigate technology-based fraud. In this course, audit analytics will be used in support of forensic investigation of technology-based fraud. Topics addressed include IT governance, enterprise risk management, IS controls, audit analytic techniques, cyber security, and data privacy. This course prepares students for future management or business advisory roles in the management, governance, and assurance of digital technologies.
learning_outcomes: Evaluate systems of IT Governance mechanisms to develop solutions that ensure the alignment of information technology capabilities with future business needs.
Explain the ethical underpinnings and legal consequences in business roles involved in providing or adopting professional independent advice.

Examine the General Control(s) and/or Application Control(s) surrounding organisational information systems for security, confidentiality, integrity, availability, accountability, efficiency, and effectiveness through the application of digital forensic techniques, audit analytics and/or controls testing.
Develop and convey recommendations for improvement using risk assessment frameworks supported with clear explanations.

Collaborate in teams to identify key insights into the nature of information systems assurance and consulting careers through reflective analysis of course materials and their relationship to professional practice.

title: Data Analytics for Business

assessments.category: Computer Code, Essay/ Critique, Practical/ Demonstration
Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Practical/ Demonstration

description: Data Analytics for Business concerns the use of data for decision making in business. It focuses on how organisations can create business value from data by modelling, integrating, analysing and visualising data. It helps students develop fundamental data analytics knowledge, experience contemporary data analytic tools and learn about broader organisational and societal implications of data analytics such as data-driven transformation and ethics of big data. Data analytics comprises skills, processes, and technologies to create timely and meaningful insights from organisational data. The insights improve managerial decision-making to create significant business value. The course explores the technical and managerial processes of using data for decision-making. It provides fundamental knowledge and skills necessary to model , integrate , analyse and visualise data. These analytical skills are highly sought after skills in various professions and industries. The course covers relevant theories as well as provides hands-on experience with business analytics tools.
learning_outcomes: Demonstrate knowledge of Data Analytics for decision-making.
Apply the principles of data management to model and integrate organisational data.
Apply data analytics techniques individually to solve business problems.
Apply data visualisation principles and techniques to represent insights from data.
Demonstrate knowledge of managerial and societal issues related to data.
topics: 1: Course Overview and Business Analytics Framework
2: Relational Databases and Intro to Data Warehousing
3: Dimensional Modelling
4: Advanced Data Warehousing Topics
5: Data Integration and Metadata
6: Performance Dashboards and Information Delivery
7: Data Analytics - Data Exploration and Fundamental Concepts

9: Data Analytics - Model Building and Best Practices
10: Big Data Management and Advanced Analytics

11: Privacy, Ethics, and Acceptable Data Use. This lecture is about big data including its definitions, its transformational impact and how it could be modelled and managed. NoSQL will be introduced as a frequently used approach to manage big data
12: Advanced Topics, Discussions, and Debates in Data Analytics. Can be a Guest Lecture

13: Course Revision and Q&A Session. This lecture discusses unintended and potentially unethical consequences of big data analytics. Scenarios of unethical data use are showed and debated in the class

title: Cyber Security Risk Management and Controls
prerequisite_raw: For MCyberSec students - CYBR7001 or CYBR7003. For all other students - at least 8 units of postgraduate courses.

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: For MCyberSec students - CYBR7001 or CYBR7003. For all other students - at least 8 units of postgraduate courses.

assessments.task: Critical Analysis of Assigned Material Online
Reflective Essay on Cybersecurity Risk Management Online

description: Cyber Security Risk Management and Controls supports future leaders in the challenging cyber security domain in understanding their role as leaders, building relationships with the Board and high-level management, and in implementing effective cyber security controls throughout the organisation. This course is designed for experienced professionals who wish to build on their ability to lead a high performance team of professional IT staff that perform cyber operations. This course will equip these professionals with the ability to evaluate and implement strong IT governance and management practices around cyber security, and the communication skills to communicate across all levels of management. The course focusses on work-relevant assessment that focusses on student roles as leaders and provide the opportunity to engage with emerging security issues, risks and vulnerabilities. Topics will include Cyber Leadership (Role of the Cyber Security Leader; Emerging Cyber Threats), Assessment and Mitigation of Risk (Preventive, Detective, Corrective Controls), IT Governance Mechanisms for Cyber Secure Organisations, IT Management Controls for Cyber Secure Organisations (Plan, Build, Run and Monitor).
learning_outcomes: Understand and act upon the different activities that characterise the management of cyber-risks in modern organisations
Make connections between cybersecurity risk management and the broader portfolio of entreprise risk management activities
Mobilise the appropriate (internal and external) resources and critical thinking to effectively perform cybersecurity risk management
Acquire skills to perform evidence-based decision-making in cybersecurity risk management
Lead organisational conversations around the importance of cybersecurity risk management and, more in general, being a champion of safe cybersecurity practices
Produce outputs in different formats (presentations, reports, etc.) to offer evidence to support decision making in the field of cybersecurity risk management

Risk management standards and exploration of their relationships with cybersecurity risk management: ISO31000; ISO27001-2. The attackers' perspective on cybersecurity risk management

Risk and audit function and cybersecurity roles Block 3: Risk and audit function within organisations: focus on cybersecurity risks (independent study session)

description: This course develops comprehension in relation to the construction regulatory environment, structural principles, construction systems and detailing, and technical documentation. Students extend knowledge of structural principles, building products and construction systems through activities and assignments that also develop skills in design detailing for small to medium-scale buildings. This course is last offered Semester 1 2026. Students should enrol in ARCH1020 Architectural Modelling and Documentation from Semester 2 2026. Please email adp@uq.edu.au for all enquiries.
learning_outcomes: Identify and interpret relevant building standards and codes.
Apply basic structural principles to a given design project.
Select and detail appropriate building products and construction systems.
Independently prepare a set of technical drawings.
Collaboratively prepare a partial construction model from a set of technical drawings.

title: Principles of Business Analytics

assessments.task: Assessment 1 - Structured Summary and Article Review  Online
Assessment 2 - Case Study Report and Poster Presentation  Online

description: Data science is transforming business and the ways in which the applied business disciplines are practiced. The field of business analytics has emerged as a result of application of the application of data science to business problems. This course introduces students to the fundamental principles of business analytics. The course aims to firstly, provide students with an appreciation of how business analytics are changing business practices and second, to offer a specific overview of key topics in business analytics including predictive modelling. The success of businesses such as Google, Netflix, and WeChat tells us the world of business is changing and becoming increasingly data-driven. The emergence of specialist consulting firms like Quantium and the development of practice areas in business analytics by the "big four" consulting firms are also emblematic of this shift in business thinking and practice -- reflecting the growing importance of analytics to many businesses. The purpose of the Business Analytics major is to equip students with the data and statistical literacy increasingly required to succeed in modern business environments.
learning_outcomes: Recognise and explain the importance of business analytics to managerial decision making
Explain the basic concepts used in business analytics and their applications to managerial decision making
Identify and apply the appropriate methods of business analytics for solving a range of business problems
Compare and critically evaluate the various approaches to business analytics, and
Evaluate the value of business analytics for improving managerial and strategic decision making.

title: Data Visualisation for Business
description: The R environment provides data scientists with powerful methods for data visualisation that aid in communicating and implementing the knowledge gained from business analytics. This course provides students of business analytics with an overview of the types of data visualisation methods available in R. A range of topics are introduced including basic plots, higher-dimensional plots, visualising text, and creating applications in R. Businesses today are awash in data and are increasingly looking for ways to leverage their data to improve their decision making, business processes, strategies, and financial performance. Data visualisation can play a crucial role in helping businesses to leverage the value of their data to achieve the outcomes they desire. For example, the visualisation of data can help business analysts to see patterns in data that are not discernible from directly interrogating the raw data and sometimes easily identified by analyses that aggregate and summarise broad patterns in the data. The visualisation of data allows for potentially large amounts of data to be presented in compact forms and in forms that analysts and other users can readily process, understand, and communicate to others. Acting on the results and making business decisions on the basis of an analysis often requires clear communication and shared understandings. For these reasons, data visualisation can be key to leveraging the power of "big data" to improve business decisions, processes, and strategies. This course provides an overview of the methods of data visualisation and exposure to the technologies and software used for data visualisation in business.
learning_outcomes: Recognise and explain the role of data visualisation for business,
Explain the key concepts in data visualisation for business,
Apply the methods of data visualisation to business,
Compare and critically evaluate the methods of data visualisation, and
Demonstrate how data visualisation can inform and improve business performance.

title: Methods of Business Analytics
prerequisite_raw: Maths B or Maths C or MATH1040

prerequisite_parsed.unparsed: Maths B or Maths C or MATH1040

description: This course introduces students to R for the purpose of business analytics and to basic programming concepts used in R. The focus is on an introduction to R, including R infrastructure and interfaces, manipulating and exploring data, predictive models, data export and output, and optimising R code. The book/film Moneyball by Michael Lewis has captured the popular imagination – showing the value of analytics of improving decision making and achieving a desired outcome. Moneyball illustrates the application of analytics to professional sports, but there are many more applications and none more important than the application of analytics to business. The application of analytics can significantly improve organisational outcomes across all aspects of business – accounting/finance, people/talent, operations, marketing, social media, and supply chain. The course BSAN2204 Methods of Business Analytics is the first course in the business analytics major after the course BSAN2201 Principles of Business Analytics. The focus of the course is the development of skills in using R – preparing students for and equipping them with the skills they need to complete the business analytics major and to pursue careers with the analytics advantage.
learning_outcomes: Recognise and explain the role of R for business analytics.
Explain the basic concepts used in R for managing and manipulating data.
Apply R for basic business analytics tasks, including data visualisation and predictive analytics/forecasting.
Compare and critically evaluate competing methods of business analytics.
Demonstrate how business analytics can inform and improve managerial decision making.
